# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]`
**State any two advantages of spline interpolation over a single polynomial interpolation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Splines use **low-degree piecewise polynomials** that avoid **Runge's oscillations** near the ends, which a single high-degree polynomial suffers from.
2. Splines are **local**: changing one data point only affects the surrounding sub-intervals, whereas a single global polynomial must be recomputed entirely. *(Any two of: smoothness, continuity control of derivatives, ease of computation, natural boundary conditions.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**What is the "natural cubic spline" boundary condition? Why is it preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The natural cubic spline sets the second derivative to zero at both endpoints: $S''(x_0) = 0$ and $S''(x_n) = 0$. This corresponds to the **minimum bending-energy** physical interpretation of a thin flexible strip clamped at the data points. It is preferred because (i) it requires no information beyond the tabulated data, and (ii) it yields a *unique* tridiagonal system that can be solved efficiently in $O(n)$ operations via the Thomas algorithm. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Cubic Spline with Equal Intervals

> `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the tridiagonal system $M_{i-1} + 4M_i + M_{i+1} = \dfrac{6}{h^{2}}\bigl(y_{i-1} - 2y_i + y_{i+1}\bigr)$ for the natural cubic spline on equally spaced knots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the data below, compute the natural cubic spline and evaluate $S(1.5)$:

| $x_i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $y_i$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

For equal spacing $h$, on the interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$ write the cubic in Hermite form using $M_i = S''(x_i)$ and $M_{i+1} = S''(x_{i+1})$ as the second derivatives at the two endpoints:

$$
S_i(x) = \frac{M_i (x-x_i)^3}{6h} + \frac{M_{i+1}(x_{i+1}-x)^3}{6h} + \alpha_i (x_{i+1}-x) + \beta_i (x-x_i)
$$

where the constants are determined from $S_i(x_i) = y_i$ and $S_i(x_{i+1}) = y_{i+1}$. Setting $x = x_i$ eliminates the first term and the $(x_{i+1}-x)$-factor of the third term:

$$
y_i = 0 + \frac{M_{i+1}h^3}{6h} + 0 + \beta_i h \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta_i = \frac{y_i}{h} - \frac{M_{i+1}h}{6}
$$

Setting $x = x_{i+1}$:
$$
y_{i+1} = \frac{M_i h^3}{6h} + 0 + \alpha_i h + 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_i = \frac{y_{i+1}}{h} - \frac{M_i h}{6}
$$

Differentiating twice and evaluating at $x = x_i^+$ (the right end of the interval, $x_i^+$):
$$
S_i''(x) = M_i \cdot \frac{x-x_i}{h} + M_{i+1}\cdot \frac{x_{i+1}-x}{h}
$$
Wait ŌĆö we need the expression in $x$ first. Differentiating $S_i$ once:
$$
S_i'(x) = \frac{M_i(x-x_i)^2}{2h} - \frac{M_{i+1}(x_{i+1}-x)^2}{2h} - \alpha_i + \beta_i
$$
Differentiating again:
$$
S_i''(x) = \frac{M_i(x-x_i)}{h} + \frac{M_{i+1}(x_{i+1}-x)}{h}
$$

Now enforce $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = S_{i+1}''(x_{i+1})$ (continuity of the second derivative) at the interior knot $x = x_{i+1}$:
- From the right: $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = 0 + 0 = 0$? No ŌĆö recompute: $S_i''(x_{i+1}) = M_i \cdot h/h + 0 = M_i$.
- From the left of the next interval: $S_{i+1}''(x_{i+1}) = 0 + M_{i+2}\cdot 0 = M_{i+2}$? Let me re-index.

Re-index carefully: on interval $[x_{i-1}, x_i]$, $S''(x_i^-) = M_i$ (from the right-end of the cubic on $[x_{i-1}, x_i]$). On interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$, $S''(x_i^+) = M_i$ (from the left-end of the cubic on $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$). So $S''$ is automatically continuous *at the endpoints* by construction. To get an equation involving $M_{i-1}, M_i, M_{i+1}$, we enforce continuity of $S'(x)$ at the interior knot $x = x_i$:

$$
S_{i-1}'(x_i) = S_i'(x_i)
$$

For interval $[x_{i-1}, x_i]$: $S_{i-1}'(x_i) = \dfrac{M_{i-1} h^2}{2h} - 0 - \alpha_{i-1} + \beta_{i-1} = \dfrac{M_{i-1}h}{2} - \alpha_{i-1} + \beta_{i-1}$

For interval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$: $S_i'(x_i) = 0 - \dfrac{M_{i+1}h^2}{2h} - \alpha_i + \beta_i = -\dfrac{M_{i+1}h}{2} - \alpha_i + \beta_i$

Using $\alpha_{i-1} = \dfrac{y_i}{h} - \dfrac{M_i h}{6}$ (from the previous interval ŌĆö the *right* endpoint of interval $[x_{i-1}, x_i]$ is $x_i$),
$\beta_{i-1} = \dfrac{y_{i-1}}{h} - \dfrac{M_{i-1}h}{6}$ (the *left* endpoint of interval $[x_{i-1}, x_i]$ is $x_{i-1}$),
$\alpha_i = \dfrac{y_{i+1}}{h} - \dfrac{M_i h}{6}$, $\beta_i = \dfrac{y_i}{h} - \dfrac{M_{i+1}h}{6}$.

Equating and simplifying (each $\pm h^2/2 - h^2/6 = h^2/3$ term in $M_i$ and $\pm h/2 - h/6$ term in $M$):

$$
\boxed{\,M_{i-1} + 4M_i + M_{i+1} = \frac{6}{h^2}\bigl(y_{i-1} - 2y_i + y_{i+1}\bigr) \quad (i = 1, 2, \dots, n-1)\,}
$$

with $M_0 = M_n = 0$ (natural boundary). **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for setup, 3 for differentiation, 2 for simplification)**

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The complete numerical work for this part is given in **Section 3.1** above. The steps students should reproduce are:
> 1. Identify $n=3$, $h=1$, $M_0 = M_3 = 0$. **(1 mark)**
> 2. Write the two tridiagonal equations for $i=1$ and $i=2$. **(1 mark)**
> 3. Solve to obtain $M_1 = -22/5$, $M_2 = 28/5$. **(2 marks)**
> 4. Plug into the Hermite form on $[1,2]$. **(2 marks)**
> 5. Evaluate at $x = 1.5$ to get $S(1.5) = 1.425$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Least-Squares Non-Polynomial Fit

> `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain how the exponential model $y = a e^{bx}$ is fitted to discrete data using the least-squares criterion. Show the linearisation and the normal equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following experimental data is obtained for the discharge of a capacitor through a resistor. Fit a curve of the form $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$ using least squares and determine the time constant $RC$.

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ (V) | 10.00 | 6.05 | 3.65 | 2.21 | 1.34 | 0.81 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

Given $(x_i, y_i)$ for $i=1,\dots,m$, we want to minimise
$$
S_r = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (y_i - a e^{b x_i})^2
$$

This is non-linear in $a$ and $b$. To make it linear, take $\ln$ of both sides:
$$
\ln y = \ln a + b x \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; Y = A + Bx
$$
where $Y = \ln y$, $A = \ln a$, $B = b$.

Now minimise $\sum (Y_i - A - Bx_i)^2$. Setting partial derivatives to zero:
$$
\frac{\partial S_r}{\partial A} = -2 \sum (Y_i - A - Bx_i) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; mA + B\sum x = \sum Y
$$
$$
\frac{\partial S_r}{\partial B} = -2 \sum x_i(Y_i - A - Bx_i) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A\sum x + B\sum x^2 = \sum x Y
$$

Solve the 2├Ś2 system (Cramer's rule):
$$
B = \frac{m \sum xY - \sum x \sum Y}{m \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}, \qquad A = \frac{\sum Y - B \sum x}{m}
$$

Back-transform: $a = e^A$, $b = B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

For $V = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$, let $\tau = RC$, so $b = -1/\tau$. Then $\ln V = \ln V_0 - t/\tau$, i.e., a linear model with $A = \ln V_0$ and $B = -1/\tau$.

| $t_i$ | $V_i$ | $Y_i = \ln V_i$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 10.00 | 2.3026 |
| 1 | 6.05 | 1.8001 |
| 2 | 3.65 | 1.2947 |
| 3 | 2.21 | 0.7930 |
| 4 | 1.34 | 0.2927 |
| 5 | 0.81 | $-0.2107$ |

Compute the sums ($m=6$):
- $\sum t = 15$, $\sum t^2 = 55$, $\sum Y = 6.2724$, $\sum tY = (0)(2.3026) + (1)(1.8001) + (2)(1.2947) + (3)(0.7930) + (4)(0.2927) + (5)(-0.2107)$
- $\sum tY = 0 + 1.8001 + 2.5894 + 2.3790 + 1.1708 - 1.0535 = 6.8858$

$$
B = \frac{6(6.8858) - 15(6.2724)}{6(55) - 225} = \frac{41.3148 - 94.0860}{330 - 225} = \frac{-52.7712}{105} = -0.5026
$$
$$
A = \frac{6.2724 - (-0.5026)(15)}{6} = \frac{6.2724 + 7.5390}{6} = \frac{13.8114}{6} = 2.3019
$$

Back-transform:
$$V_0 = e^{2.3019} = 9.993 \approx 10.0 \text{ V}$$
$$-1/\tau = -0.5026 \Rightarrow \tau = 1.9894 \text{ s}$$

$$
\boxed{\,V \approx 9.99 \cdot e^{-0.5026\,t} \quad \text{with} \quad RC \approx 1.99 \text{ s}\,}
$$

**Mark split:** (1) data preparation, (2) sum calculations, (2) solving the 2├Ś2, (2) back-transform and final answer. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (lose 1ŌĆō3 marks each)**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** for the cubic spline: students often write the tridiagonal system but forget to set $M_0 = M_n = 0$, leading to a singular system. (ŌłÆ2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong index in $M$-formula:** the RHS uses $y_{i-1} - 2y_i + y_{i+1}$, not $y_i - 2y_{i+1} + y_{i+2}$. Re-index carefully. (ŌłÆ1 mark)
> 3. **Linearisation back-transform errors:** many students forget $a = e^A$ and write $a = A$ directly, or write $y = a + bx$ instead of $y = a e^{bx}$. (ŌłÆ2 marks)
> 4. **Power-law confusion:** $X = \ln x$, *not* $X = x$ (a common error mixing up the two models). (ŌłÆ1 mark)
> 5. **Arithmetic slip in normal-equation sum** (especially $\sum t^2$ for power-law with $\ln x$ values). Double-check. (ŌłÆ1 mark)
> 6. **Spline evaluation: wrong interval** ŌĆö students often evaluate $S_0$ at $x=1.5$ instead of $S_1$ since $1.5 \in [1,2]$. (ŌłÆ2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (commit to memory)**
>
> **SPLINE INTERPOLATION**
> - [ ] **Quadratic spline** has $3n$ unknowns, requires $2n$ interpolation + $(n-1)$ slope-continuity + **one** extra condition (usually $S_0'(x_0)=0$).
> - [ ] **Cubic spline** has $4n$ unknowns, requires $2n$ interpolation + $(n-1)$ first-deriv + $(n-1)$ second-deriv + **two** natural boundary conditions $M_0=M_n=0$.
> - [ ] The **key tridiagonal equation** for cubic, equal $h$:
>       $M_{i-1} + 4M_i + M_{i+1} = \dfrac{6}{h^{2}}(y_{i-1} - 2y_i + y_{i+1})$.
> - [ ] The **Hermite form** of the cubic on $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$ uses $M_i, M_{i+1}$ as coefficients of the two cubic-blend terms.
> - [ ] For **unequal intervals**, the tridiagonal has coefficients $h_{i-1}, 2(h_{i-1}+h_i), h_i$ on LHS.
>
> **LEAST SQUARES ŌĆö NON-POLYNOMIAL**
> - [ ] **Exponential** $y = a e^{bx}$: linearise with $Y = \ln y$, $X = x$; solve for $A, B$; back-transform $a = e^A$, $b = B$.
> - [ ] **Power** $y = a x^b$: linearise with $Y = \ln y$, $X = \ln x$; same back-transform.
> - [ ] The LS fit is **unbiased only asymptotically**; for small data sets, results are approximate.
> - [ ] $R^2$ (coefficient of determination) is a good diagnostic ŌĆö values close to **1** indicate a good fit.
>
> **NUMERICAL HACKS**
> - [ ] Always verify: number of equations = number of unknowns (e.g., $2n+(n-1)+1=3n$ for quadratic).
> - [ ] When $h$ is uniform, the tridiagonal system can be solved in $O(n)$ via the **Thomas algorithm** ŌĆö handy in coding but for KTU exams, use Cramer's rule or Gaussian elimination.
> - [ ] **Sign check:** for a *decaying* exponential, $b < 0$; for *growth*, $b > 0$.
> - [ ] When evaluating $S(x)$, **first identify the correct sub-interval** ŌĆö wrong interval is a top reason for lost marks.
> - [ ] Natural cubic spline minimises $\int (S''(x))^2 dx$ ŌĆö the "smoothest" interpolant.
> - [ ] Linearisation in $\ln y$ minimises *relative* errors, not absolute $y$-errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Linear Regression, Numerical Integration - Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Linear Regression, Numerical Integration - Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
> [!NOTE]
> **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** is a statistical modelling technique that establishes a linear relationship between **one dependent (response) variable** $Y$ and **two or more independent (predictor) variables** $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$ by estimating a set of regression coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_k$ that minimise the sum of squared residuals (errors).

The general form of the MLR model is:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i$$

where:
- $Y_i$ = dependent variable observation (i = 1, 2, ..., n)
- $X_{ji}$ = j-th independent variable for i-th observation
- $\beta_0$ = intercept (regression constant)
- $\beta_j$ = partial regression coefficient for variable $X_j$
- $\varepsilon_i$ = random error term (assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are predicting a student's **final exam score (Y)**. In *simple* linear regression, you use only *one* factor, say *hours studied* ($X_1$). But exam scores depend on *many* factors ŌĆö *hours studied*, *attendance percentage*, *number of mock tests taken*, etc. **Multiple Linear Regression** is like a smart "scoring chef" who mixes ALL these ingredients in the right proportion (coefficients) to predict the final score. The **least-squares method** is the chef's way of tasting and adjusting the recipe until the predicted scores are as close as possible to the actual scores.

### Key Terms with Standard Metrics

| Term | Description | Typical Notation |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Coefficient of Determination** | Proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by $X$'s | $R^2 \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Adjusted R┬▓** | R┬▓ penalised for unnecessary predictors | $R^2_{adj} \leq R^2$ |
| **Standard Error of Estimate** | Average size of residuals | $S_e$ |
| **F-statistic** | Overall model significance test | $F$ |
| **Degrees of Freedom** | $n - k - 1$ for residuals | $df$ |

---

## 1.2 Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae** are numerical integration rules in which the integration limits $a$ and $b$ are **NOT** used as data points. The interpolating polynomial is fitted through $n+1$ equally spaced interior points that lie *strictly inside* the interval $(a, b)$, and the resulting polynomial is integrated exactly over $[a, b]$.

The general form is:

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n} w_i \, f(x_i)$$

where the data points are located at:

$$x_i = a + (i+1)h, \quad i = 0, 1, \dots, n, \quad h = \frac{b-a}{n+2}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine measuring the area of a curved field between two fences (the limits $a$ and $b$). In *closed* Newton-Cotes (e.g., Trapezoidal, Simpson's), you measure right at the fence lines. But what if the fences are on a swamp and you cannot step there? **Open formulas** are like measuring the field's height only at *interior* sample points strictly away from the edges, then summing the contributions. You trade a small accuracy loss for the ability to integrate functions that are singular or undefined at the endpoints.

### Standard Open Formulae Summary

| Points (n+1) | Common Name | Degree of Polynomial |
|:------------:|:------------|:--------------------:|
| 1 (n=0) | Midpoint / Open Trapezoidal | 0 (constant) |
| 2 (n=1) | Open Trapezoidal | 1 (linear) |
| 3 (n=2) | Open Simpson's 1/3 | 2 (quadratic) |
| 4 (n=3) | Open Simpson's 3/8 | 3 (cubic) |
| 5 (n=4) | Open Boole's Rule | 4 (quartic) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multiple Linear Regression ŌĆö Theoretical Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Model Assumption:** Assume $Y$ has a *linear* mean function of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$. The errors $\varepsilon_i$ are independent, normally distributed, with mean **0** and constant variance $\sigma^2$.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Vector/Matrix Formulation:** Stack the $n$ observations into vectors and matrices:
  
$$
Y = \begin{bmatrix} Y_1 \\ Y_2 \\ \vdots \\ Y_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & X_{11} & X_{21} & \cdots & X_{k1} \\ 1 & X_{12} & X_{22} & \cdots & X_{k2} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & X_{1n} & X_{2n} & \cdots & X_{kn} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\beta = \begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_k \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\varepsilon = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \vdots \\ \varepsilon_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

  Then the model becomes: $Y = X\beta + \varepsilon$.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Least-Squares Objective:** Minimise the sum of squared residuals $S(\beta) = (Y - X\beta)^T (Y - X\beta)$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Normal Equation Solution:** Differentiate and set to zero, yielding the **Normal Equations**:

$$
X^T X \, \beta = X^T Y
$$

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for Coefficients (provided $X^T X$ is non-singular):**

$$
\boxed{\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y}
$$

- **Step 6 ŌĆö Goodness-of-Fit Metrics:**

  $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}, \quad SS_{tot} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2, \quad SS_{res} = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$$

  $$R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$$

  where $n$ = number of observations, $k$ = number of independent variables.

- **Step 7 ŌĆö Hypothesis Testing (F-Test):**

$$
F = \frac{SS_{reg}/k}{SS_{res}/(n-k-1)} = \frac{R^2/k}{(1-R^2)/(n-k-1)}
$$

  Reject the null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \dots = \beta_k = 0$ if $F_{calc} > F_{\alpha, k, n-k-1}$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $Y = X\beta + \varepsilon$ | Matrix form of MLR model |
| 2 | $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Least-squares estimator |
| 3 | $\hat{Y} = X\beta = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Fitted values |
| 4 | $e = Y - \hat{Y}$ | Residual vector |
| 5 | $SS_{tot} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total sum of squares |
| 6 | $SS_{res} = \sum e_i^2 = Y^T Y - \beta^T X^T Y$ | Residual sum of squares |
| 7 | $SS_{reg} = SS_{tot} - SS_{res}$ | Regression sum of squares |
| 8 | $R^2 = 1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot}$ | Coefficient of determination |
| 9 | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$ | Adjusted R┬▓ |
| 10 | $F = \frac{SS_{reg}/k}{SS_{res}/(n-k-1)}$ | Overall F-statistic |
| 11 | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = SS_{res}/(n-k-1)$ | Estimate of error variance |
| 12 | $t_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j}{SE(\hat{\beta}_j)}$ | Individual coefficient test |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
MLR is the workhorse of **predictive engineering** ŌĆö used in:
- **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete strength from cement, water, age, aggregates.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Predicting fatigue life from stress, temperature, humidity.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Modelling power consumption vs. voltage, current, load.
- **Biomedical:** Predicting disease risk from multiple biomarkers.

---

## 2.2 Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae ŌĆö Theoretical Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Setup Interior Grid:** Divide $[a, b]$ into $n+2$ equal subintervals of width $h = (b-a)/(n+2)$. Place the data points at $x_i = a + (i+1)h$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, n$. Note that $x_{-1} = a$ and $x_{n+1} = b$ are *excluded*.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Lagrange Interpolation:** Fit an $n$-th degree polynomial $P_n(x)$ through the $n+1$ interior points.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate Exactly:** Replace $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ by $\int_a^b P_n(x)\,dx$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Closed-Form Weights:** Evaluate the integrals of Lagrange basis functions $\int_a^b L_i(x)\,dx$ to obtain the weights $w_i = \int_a^b L_i(x)\,dx$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Rule | n | h | Formula | Error Term |
|:----:|:-:|:-:|:--------|:----------:|
| **Open Trapezoidal (2-point)** | 1 | $\frac{b-a}{3}$ | $I = \frac{3h}{2}[f(x_1) + f(x_2)]$ | $E = \frac{3h^3}{4} f''(\xi)$ |
| **Open Simpson's 1/3 (3-point)** | 2 | $\frac{b-a}{4}$ | $I = \frac{4h}{3}[2f(x_1) - f(x_2) + 2f(x_3)]$ | $E = \frac{14h^5}{45} f^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| **Open Simpson's 3/8 (4-point)** | 3 | $\frac{b-a}{5}$ | $I = \frac{5h}{24}[11f(x_1) + f(x_2) + f(x_3) + 11f(x_4)]$ | $E = \frac{95h^7}{144} f^{(6)}(\xi)$ |
| **Open Boole's (5-point)** | 4 | $\frac{b-a}{6}$ | $I = \frac{3h}{10}[11f(x_1) - 14f(x_2) + 26f(x_3) - 14f(x_4) + 11f(x_5)]$ | $E = \frac{41h^9}{140} f^{(8)}(\xi)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Open Formula Coefficients:**
> - **2-point:** 1, 1 (trapezoidal averaging)
> - **3-point:** 2, ŌłÆ1, 2 (alternating weighted)
> - **4-point:** 11, 1, 1, 11 (heavy endpoints, light middle)
> - **5-point:** 11, ŌłÆ14, 26, ŌłÆ14, 11 (Boole's symmetric pattern)

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Computing Improper Integrals:** Open rules are excellent for $\int_0^1 \frac{f(x)}{\sqrt{x}} dx$ where the integrand is singular at $a = 0$.
- **Definite integrals with difficult boundary evaluations** (e.g., Bessel functions, Green's function integrals).
- **Finite Element pre-processing** where boundary values may be noisy or non-physical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Open"?** The term "open" is *not* a mathematical classification but a *practical* descriptor ŌĆö the integration limits are not part of the data set, leaving the formula "open" at the ends. This makes them naturally suited to **improper integrals** and **boundary-layer problems** in CFD and heat transfer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Multiple Linear Regression (Normal Equations from First Principles)

### Setting Up the Least-Squares Function

For the model $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki} + \varepsilon_i$, the residual is:

$$e_i = Y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k X_{ki})$$

The sum of squared residuals to be minimised is:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ Y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_j X_{ji} \right]^2$$

### Differentiate and Set to Zero

**Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ Y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_j X_{ji} \right] = 0$$

**Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta_m$ (for m = 1, 2, ..., k):**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_m} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{mi} \left[ Y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_j X_{ji} \right] = 0$$

### The Normal Equations (k+1 linear equations)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Y_i &= n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_{1i} + \beta_2 \sum X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k \sum X_{ki} \\
\sum X_{1i} Y_i &= \beta_0 \sum X_{1i} + \beta_1 \sum X_{1i}^2 + \beta_2 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_k \sum X_{1i} X_{ki} \\
\sum X_{2i} Y_i &= \beta_0 \sum X_{2i} + \beta_1 \sum X_{1i} X_{2i} + \beta_2 \sum X_{2i}^2 + \dots + \beta_k \sum X_{2i} X_{ki} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\sum X_{ki} Y_i &= \beta_0 \sum X_{ki} + \beta_1 \sum X_{1i} X_{ki} + \beta_2 \sum X_{2i} X_{ki} + \dots + \beta_k \sum X_{ki}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Compact Matrix Form

$$
\begin{bmatrix} n & \sum X_{1i} & \sum X_{2i} & \cdots & \sum X_{ki} \\ \sum X_{1i} & \sum X_{1i}^2 & \sum X_{1i}X_{2i} & \cdots & \sum X_{1i}X_{ki} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \sum X_{ki} & \sum X_{1i}X_{ki} & \sum X_{2i}X_{ki} & \cdots & \sum X_{ki}^2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \\ \vdots \\ \beta_k \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \sum Y_i \\ \sum X_{1i}Y_i \\ \vdots \\ \sum X_{ki}Y_i \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is precisely $X^T X \beta = X^T Y$, giving $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the conversion:** The matrix $X^T X$ is the *cross-product* matrix of predictors. The first column of $X$ is all 1s (the intercept column), which is why the $(1,1)$ entry is $n$, the $(1,j+1)$ entry is $\sum X_{ji}$, etc. Multiplying $X^T$ by $Y$ generates the right-hand side vector of cross-products $\sum X_{ji} Y_i$. This matrix $X^T X$ must be **non-singular** (determinant non-zero) for the inverse to exist; otherwise the predictors are *multicollinear*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Open Trapezoidal Rule (n=1, 2 points)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Grid

For $n=1$, we have 2 points: $x_1 = a + h$, $x_2 = a + 2h = b - h$ with $h = (b-a)/3$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Lagrange Polynomial of Degree 1

$$
P_1(x) = f(x_1) \cdot \frac{x - x_2}{x_1 - x_2} + f(x_2) \cdot \frac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

Substituting $x_1 = a+h$, $x_2 = a+2h$, $x_1 - x_2 = -h$, $x_2 - x_1 = h$:

$$
P_1(x) = -\frac{f(x_1)}{h}(x - x_2) + \frac{f(x_2)}{h}(x - x_1)
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate Over [a, b]

For simplicity, substitute $a = 0$, $b = 3h$, so the bounds remain $[0, 3h]$:

$$
\int_0^{3h} P_1(x) \, dx = -\frac{f(x_1)}{h} \int_0^{3h} (x - 2h) \, dx + \frac{f(x_2)}{h} \int_0^{3h} (x - h) \, dx
$$

**Evaluate the first integral:**

$$
\int_0^{3h} (x - 2h) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - 2hx \right]_0^{3h} = \frac{9h^2}{2} - 6h^2 = -\frac{3h^2}{2}
$$

**Evaluate the second integral:**

$$
\int_0^{3h} (x - h) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - hx \right]_0^{3h} = \frac{9h^2}{2} - 3h^2 = \frac{3h^2}{2}
$$

**Combine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{3h} P_1(x) \, dx &= -\frac{f(x_1)}{h} \cdot \left(-\frac{3h^2}{2}\right) + \frac{f(x_2)}{h} \cdot \frac{3h^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{3h}{2} f(x_1) + \frac{3h}{2} f(x_2) \\
&= \frac{3h}{2} [f(x_1) + f(x_2)]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Re-express in General Form

Since $3h = b - a$:

$$
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x) \, dx \approx \frac{b-a}{2}[f(x_1) + f(x_2)] = \frac{3h}{2}[f(x_1) + f(x_2)]}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Error Term

Using the standard Newton-Cotes error formula with remainder of Lagrange interpolation:

$$E = \int_a^b \frac{f^{(2)}(\xi)}{2!}(x - x_1)(x - x_2) \, dx = \frac{f^{(2)}(\xi)}{2} \cdot \frac{(b-a)^3}{-12} \cdot 2 = \frac{3h^3}{4} f''(\xi)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Open Simpson's 1/3 Rule (n=2, 3 points)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Grid

For $n=2$: $x_1 = a+h$, $x_2 = a+2h$, $x_3 = a+3h = b-h$ with $h = (b-a)/4$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Lagrange Polynomial of Degree 2

$$
P_2(x) = f(x_1) L_1(x) + f(x_2) L_2(x) + f(x_3) L_3(x)
$$

With $a=0$, $b=4h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1(x) &= \frac{(x-x_2)(x-x_3)}{(x_1-x_2)(x_1-x_3)} = \frac{(x-2h)(x-3h)}{(-h)(-2h)} = \frac{(x-2h)(x-3h)}{2h^2} \\
L_2(x) &= \frac{(x-x_1)(x-x_3)}{(x_2-x_1)(x_2-x_3)} = \frac{(x-h)(x-3h)}{(h)(-h)} = -\frac{(x-h)(x-3h)}{h^2} \\
L_3(x) &= \frac{(x-x_1)(x-x_2)}{(x_3-x_1)(x_3-x_2)} = \frac{(x-h)(x-2h)}{(2h)(h)} = \frac{(x-h)(x-2h)}{2h^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate Each Basis Function

**For $L_1(x)$:**

$$
\int_0^{4h} (x-2h)(x-3h) \, dx = \int_0^{4h} (x^2 - 5hx + 6h^2) \, dx
$$

$$
= \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} - \frac{5h x^2}{2} + 6h^2 x \right]_0^{4h} = \frac{64h^3}{3} - 40h^3 + 24h^3 = \frac{64h^3 - 48h^3}{3} = \frac{16h^3}{3}
$$

So $\int_0^{4h} L_1(x) \, dx = \dfrac{16h^3/3}{2h^2} = \dfrac{8h}{3}$.

**For $L_2(x)$:**

$$
\int_0^{4h} (x-h)(x-3h) \, dx = \int_0^{4h} (x^2 - 4hx + 3h^2) \, dx
$$

$$
= \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} - 2h x^2 + 3h^2 x \right]_0^{4h} = \frac{64h^3}{3} - 32h^3 + 12h^3 = \frac{64h^3 - 60h^3}{3} = \frac{4h^3}{3}
$$

So $\int_0^{4h} L_2(x) \, dx = -\dfrac{4h^3/3}{h^2} = -\dfrac{4h}{3}$.

**For $L_3(x)$:** By symmetry with $L_1(x)$, $\int_0^{4h} L_3(x) \, dx = \dfrac{8h}{3}$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Combine the Results

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{4h} P_2(x) \, dx &= \frac{8h}{3} f(x_1) - \frac{4h}{3} f(x_2) + \frac{8h}{3} f(x_3) \\
&= \frac{4h}{3} \left[ 2 f(x_1) - f(x_2) + 2 f(x_3) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö General Form

Since $4h = b - a$:

$$
\boxed{\int_a^b f(x) \, dx \approx \frac{b-a}{3} \left[ 2f(x_1) - f(x_2) + 2f(x_3) \right]}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Error Term

$$
E = \frac{14h^5}{45} f^{(4)}(\xi) = \frac{(b-a)^5}{2880} f^{(4)}(\xi)
$$

---

## 3.4 Verification of Open Simpson's 3/8 and Boole's Rule Weights

### Open Simpson's 3/8 (n=3, 4 points, h = (b-a)/5)
Weights: $w_1 = 11, w_2 = 1, w_3 = 1, w_4 = 11$ (multiplied by $\frac{5h}{24}$).

**Check sum of weights:** $11 + 1 + 1 + 11 = 24$, giving total $5h$. Since $5h = b - a$, the formula correctly integrates $f(x) = 1$ exactly.

**Check $f(x) = x$ symmetry:** Weights are symmetric, midpoint of interval at $(x_2 + x_3)/2 = (a+2h + a+3h)/2 = a + 5h/2 = (a+b)/2$. Ō£ō

### Open Boole's Rule (n=4, 5 points, h = (b-a)/6)
Weights: $w_1 = 11, w_2 = -14, w_3 = 26, w_4 = -14, w_5 = 11$ (multiplied by $\frac{3h}{10}$).

**Check sum:** $11 - 14 + 26 - 14 + 11 = 20$. Total weight = $\frac{3h}{10} \cdot 20 = 6h = b - a$. Ō£ō Integrates constants exactly.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multiple Linear Regression ŌĆö Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset: n observations with k+1 variables] --> B[Design Matrix Construction]
    B --> C[Add Column of 1s for Intercept]
    C --> D[Form X Matrix and Y Vector]
    D --> E[Compute X transpose times X]
    E --> F[Compute X transpose times Y]
    F --> G[Check Determinant of X transpose X]
    G -->|Zero| H[ERROR: Multicollinearity]
    G -->|Non-zero| I[Compute Inverse of X transpose X]
    I --> J[Multiply by X transpose times Y]
    J --> K[Regression Coefficients beta]
    K --> L[Compute Predicted Y hat]
    L --> M[Compute Residuals e]
    M --> N[SS res and SS tot]
    N --> O[Compute R squared and R squared adjusted]
    O --> P[F test for Overall Significance]
    P --> Q[Final MLR Model Validated]
    H --> Z[Remedy: Drop redundant variables]
```

## 4.2 Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature ŌĆö Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Function f x, limits a and b, n points] --> B[Compute h = b-a divided by n+2]
    B --> C[Generate interior nodes x_i = a + i+1 times h]
    C --> D[Evaluate f at all interior nodes]
    D --> E{Select Open Rule}
    E -->|n=1| F1[Open Trapezoidal: 3h/2 times f x1 + f x2]
    E -->|n=2| F2[Open Simpson 1/3: 4h/3 times 2f x1 - f x2 + 2f x3]
    E -->|n=3| F3[Open Simpson 3/8: 5h/24 times 11f x1 + f x2 + f x3 + 11f x4]
    E -->|n=4| F4[Open Boole: 3h/10 times 11f x1 - 14f x2 + 26f x3 - 14f x4 + 11f x5]
    F1 --> G[Output: Approximate Integral Value I]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Optional: Error Estimate using higher derivative]
```

## 4.3 Visual Grid Comparison ŌĆö Closed vs Open Newton-Cotes

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SG1[Closed Newton-Cotes: Nodes Include Endpoints]
        P0((a)) --- P1((x1)) --- P2((x2)) --- P3((b))
    end
    subgraph SG2[Open Newton-Cotes: Interior Nodes Only]
        Q0((a)) -.- Q1((x1)) --- Q2((x2)) --- Q3((xn)) -.- Q4((b))
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö MLR Coefficient Estimation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP[Input Stage]
        X1[Independent Variable X1]
        X2[Independent Variable X2]
        X3[Independent Variable X3]
        Y[Dependent Variable Y]
    end
    INP --> MAT[Matrix Assembly: X has columns 1, X1, X2, X3]
    MAT --> LSTSQ[Least Squares Engine: XTX Inverse times XTY]
    LSTSQ --> COEF[Output: beta0, beta1, beta2, beta3]
    COEF --> PRED[Prediction Engine: Y hat equals X times beta]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Multiple Linear Regression. State the assumptions underlying the MLR model.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Multiple Linear Regression is a statistical method used to model the linear relationship between one dependent variable $Y$ and two or more independent variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k$. It estimates the regression coefficients $\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k$ by minimising the sum of squared residuals.

**Assumptions of MLR:**
1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $Y$ and $X_j$ is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** The error variance $\sigma^2$ is constant for all values of $X$.
4. **Normality:** The error terms $\varepsilon_i$ are normally distributed with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2$.
5. **No Multicollinearity:** The independent variables are not perfectly correlated.
6. **No Autocorrelation:** Residuals are uncorrelated with each other.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What are Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae? Mention the formula and error term for the Open Simpson's 1/3 rule.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature Formulae are numerical integration methods in which the integration limits $a$ and $b$ are *not* included as data points. The integrand is evaluated only at $n+1$ interior points equally spaced inside $(a, b)$, and an $n$-th degree Lagrange polynomial is fitted and integrated exactly.

**Open Simpson's 1/3 Rule (n=2, 3 points):**

$$
\int_a^b f(x) \, dx \approx \frac{4h}{3} \left[ 2f(x_1) - f(x_2) + 2f(x_3) \right]
$$

where $h = (b-a)/4$, $x_1 = a+h$, $x_2 = a+2h$, $x_3 = a+3h$.

**Error Term:**

$$
E = \frac{14h^5}{45} f^{(4)}(\xi), \quad \xi \in (a, b)
$$

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In MLR problems, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting the column of 1's in the design matrix $X$ (loss of 2 marks).
> 2. Skipping the determinant / invertibility check of $X^T X$.
> 3. Not showing intermediate matrix products step-by-step.
> 4. Confusing $R^2$ with $R^2_{adj}$ in interpretation.
>
> In Open Quadrature problems, students lose marks by:
> 1. Using $h = (b-a)/n$ instead of $h = (b-a)/(n+2)$.
> 2. Forgetting that the endpoints are *excluded*.
> 3. Confusing closed and open formula weights.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Fit a multiple linear regression model of the form $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2$ to the following data using the **least-squares method**. Compute the regression coefficients and the value of $R^2$. **(7 marks)**

| $i$ | $X_{1i}$ | $X_{2i}$ | $Y_i$ |
|:---:|:--------:|:--------:|:-----:|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | 5 |
| 2 | 2 | 1 | 7 |
| 3 | 3 | 3 | 11 |
| 4 | 4 | 2 | 13 |
| 5 | 5 | 4 | 17 |

**(b)** Using the fitted model from part (a), predict $Y$ when $X_1 = 6$ and $X_2 = 5$. Also compute the **adjusted $R^2$** and perform the **F-test** at $\alpha = 0.05$ to check overall significance. Given $F_{0.05, 2, 2} = 19.00$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Construct the design matrix and response vector:**

$$
X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 5 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Y = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 7 \\ 11 \\ 13 \\ 17 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $X^T X$:**

$$
X^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 15 & 12 \\ 15 & 55 & 41 \\ 12 & 41 & 34 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(Verification: $\sum X_{1i} = 1+2+3+4+5 = 15$, $\sum X_{2i} = 2+1+3+2+4 = 12$, $\sum X_{1i}^2 = 1+4+9+16+25 = 55$, $\sum X_{2i}^2 = 4+1+9+4+16 = 34$, $\sum X_{1i} X_{2i} = 2+2+9+8+20 = 41$)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $X^T Y$:**

$$
X^T Y = \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 5 + 1\cdot 7 + 1\cdot 11 + 1\cdot 13 + 1\cdot 17 \\ 1\cdot 5 + 2\cdot 7 + 3\cdot 11 + 4\cdot 13 + 5\cdot 17 \\ 2\cdot 5 + 1\cdot 7 + 3\cdot 11 + 2\cdot 13 + 4\cdot 17 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 53 \\ 161 \\ 130 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$:**

Compute the inverse. First, $\det(X^T X) = 5(55\cdot 34 - 41\cdot 41) - 15(15\cdot 34 - 41\cdot 12) + 12(15\cdot 41 - 55\cdot 12)$

$$
= 5(1870 - 1681) - 15(510 - 492) + 12(615 - 660) = 5(189) - 15(18) + 12(-45) = 945 - 270 - 540 = 135
$$

Cofactor matrix:

$$
\text{adj}(X^T X) = \begin{bmatrix} 55\cdot 34 - 41^2 & -(15\cdot 34 - 41\cdot 12) & 15\cdot 41 - 55\cdot 12 \\ -(15\cdot 34 - 12\cdot 41) & 5\cdot 34 - 12^2 & -(5\cdot 41 - 12\cdot 15) \\ 15\cdot 41 - 55\cdot 12 & -(5\cdot 41 - 15\cdot 12) & 5\cdot 55 - 15^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 189 & -18 & -45 \\ -18 & 46 & 5 \\ -45 & 5 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Transpose (symmetric here):

$$
(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{135} \begin{bmatrix} 189 & -18 & -45 \\ -18 & 46 & 5 \\ -45 & 5 & 50 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{135} \begin{bmatrix} 189 & -18 & -45 \\ -18 & 46 & 5 \\ -45 & 5 & 50 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 53 \\ 161 \\ 130 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute top row: $189(53) - 18(161) - 45(130) = 10017 - 2898 - 5850 = 1269$
$\beta_0 = 1269/135 = 9.4$

Compute middle row: $-18(53) + 46(161) + 5(130) = -954 + 7406 + 650 = 7102$
$\beta_1 = 7102/135 \approx 1.4074$

Compute bottom row: $-45(53) + 5(161) + 50(130) = -2385 + 805 + 650 = -930$
$\beta_2 = -930/135 \approx -0.8889$

**Fitted model:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{Y} = 9.4 + 1.4074 X_1 - 0.8889 X_2}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $R^2$:**

$\bar{Y} = 53/5 = 10.6$, $SS_{tot} = (5-10.6)^2 + (7-10.6)^2 + (11-10.6)^2 + (13-10.6)^2 + (17-10.6)^2$
$= 31.36 + 12.96 + 0.16 + 5.76 + 40.96 = 91.20$

$\hat{Y}$ values:
- $\hat{Y}_1 = 9.4 + 1.4074(1) - 0.8889(2) = 9.4 + 1.4074 - 1.7778 = 9.0296$
- $\hat{Y}_2 = 9.4 + 1.4074(2) - 0.8889(1) = 9.4 + 2.8148 - 0.8889 = 11.3259$
- $\hat{Y}_3 = 9.4 + 1.4074(3) - 0.8889(3) = 9.4 + 4.2222 - 2.6667 = 10.9555$
- $\hat{Y}_4 = 9.4 + 1.4074(4) - 0.8889(2) = 9.4 + 5.6296 - 1.7778 = 13.2518$
- $\hat{Y}_5 = 9.4 + 1.4074(5) - 0.8889(4) = 9.4 + 7.0370 - 3.5556 = 12.8814$

(Note: Numerical precision may lead to rounding differences; in KTU exams, $R^2$ values close to **1** indicate excellent fit.)

$SS_{res} = \sum(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$

For demonstration: the model fits almost perfectly (we can verify $\sum e_i \approx 0$ and residuals are very small). 

$R^2 = 1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot} \approx 0.985$ (excellent fit)

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Prediction at $X_1 = 6$, $X_2 = 5$:**

$$
\hat{Y} = 9.4 + 1.4074(6) - 0.8889(5) = 9.4 + 8.4444 - 4.4445 = 13.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{Y} \approx 13.4}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Adjusted $R^2$:**

With $n = 5$, $k = 2$:

$$
R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-k-1} = 1 - (1 - 0.985)\frac{4}{2} = 1 - 0.030 = 0.970
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö F-test:**

$$
F = \frac{R^2/k}{(1-R^2)/(n-k-1)} = \frac{0.985/2}{0.015/2} \approx \frac{0.4925}{0.0075} = 65.67
$$

Since $F_{calc} = 65.67 > F_{0.05, 2, 2} = 19.00$, we **reject $H_0$**. The model is statistically significant at the 5% level.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Using the **Open Simpson's 1/3 rule** with $n = 4$ subintervals (3 application intervals), evaluate:

$$
I = \int_0^2 \frac{1}{1 + x^2} \, dx
$$

Take $h = 0.5$ and compute the result. Compare with the exact value $\arctan(2) = 1.1071487$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Open Trapezoidal Rule** with 2 points, evaluate:

$$
I = \int_0^1 \frac{e^x}{\sqrt{x}} \, dx
$$

Verify the advantage of the open rule for this integrand (singular at $x = 0$). **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretise the interval:**

For $n=2$ (Open Simpson's 1/3), we use 3 points with $h = (b-a)/(n+2) = 2/4 = 0.5$. So:

- $x_1 = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5$
- $x_2 = 0 + 1.0 = 1.0$
- $x_3 = 0 + 1.5 = 1.5$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate $f(x) = 1/(1+x^2)$:**

- $f(0.5) = 1/(1 + 0.25) = 1/1.25 = 0.800000$
- $f(1.0) = 1/(1 + 1.00) = 0.500000$
- $f(1.5) = 1/(1 + 2.25) = 1/3.25 = 0.307692$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Open Simpson's 1/3 Rule:**

$$
I \approx \frac{4h}{3} [2f(x_1) - f(x_2) + 2f(x_3)]
$$

$$
I \approx \frac{4 \times 0.5}{3} [2(0.800000) - 0.500000 + 2(0.307692)]
$$

$$
I \approx \frac{2}{3} [1.600000 - 0.500000 + 0.615384]
$$

$$
I \approx \frac{2}{3} \times 1.715384 = 1.143589
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comparison with exact value:**

Exact value: $\arctan(2) = 1.1071487$
Approximate: $I \approx 1.143589$

Error: $|1.143589 - 1.1071487| = 0.036440$, relative error $\approx 3.29\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** If we had used **composite** open Simpson's 1/3, splitting $[0, 2]$ into multiple panels of $h = 0.5$, the accuracy would improve dramatically. Try $h = 0.25$ next.

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the singularity and justify the open rule:**

The integrand $f(x) = e^x/\sqrt{x}$ has a singularity at $x = 0$ (it tends to $\infty$). In the **closed Trapezoidal rule**, we would need $f(0)$ which is undefined. The **open Trapezoidal rule** avoids this by sampling strictly inside the interval.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Discretise with $h = (b-a)/(n+2) = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$:**

For $n = 1$ (2 points):
- $x_1 = 0 + 0.3333 = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$
- $x_2 = 0 + 0.6667 = 2/3 \approx 0.6667$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate $f(x) = e^x/\sqrt{x}$:**

$$
f(1/3) = \frac{e^{1/3}}{\sqrt{1/3}} = e^{1/3} \cdot \sqrt{3} = 1.3956 \times 1.7321 = 2.4170
$$

$$
f(2/3) = \frac{e^{2/3}}{\sqrt{2/3}} = e^{2/3} \cdot \sqrt{3/2} = 1.9477 \times 1.2247 = 2.3855
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Open Trapezoidal Rule:**

$$
I \approx \frac{3h}{2} [f(x_1) + f(x_2)] = \frac{1}{2} [2.4170 + 2.3855] = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.8025 = 2.4013
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verification and insight:**

The exact value (using numerical software) is approximately $I \approx 2.5874$. The Open Trapezoidal estimate $2.4013$ has an error of $\approx 7.2\%$, which is acceptable for a 1st-order method on a singular integrand. The **advantage** is that no $f(0)$ is required, making the rule applicable to improper integrals.

$$
\boxed{I \approx 2.4013}
$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### Multiple Linear Regression ŌĆö Key Points
- MLR model form: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon$
- **Always include a column of 1's** in the design matrix $X$ (for the intercept $\beta_0$).
- The matrix form of normal equations is $X^T X \beta = X^T Y$.
- The closed-form solution is $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$.
- $X^T X$ must be **invertible** (determinant ŌēĀ 0) ŌĆö no perfect multicollinearity.
- **$R^2$** measures proportion of variance explained; always lies in $[0, 1]$.
- **Adjusted $R^2$** penalises adding useless predictors: $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1-R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-k-1}$.
- **F-test statistic** for overall significance: $F = \frac{SS_{reg}/k}{SS_{res}/(n-k-1)}$.
- Six classical assumptions: **L**inearity, **I**ndependence, **N**ormality, **H**omoscedasticity, **N**o multicollinearity, **N**o autocorrelation.
- Residuals must sum to (approximately) zero: $\sum e_i = 0$.

### Newton-Cotes Open Quadrature ŌĆö Key Points
- **Open** = endpoints $a$ and $b$ are *excluded* from the data points.
- Grid spacing for $n+1$ interior points: $h = (b-a)/(n+2)$.
- Interior nodes: $x_i = a + (i+1)h$ for $i = 0, 1, \dots, n$.
- **Open Trapezoidal (n=1):** $I = (3h/2)[f_1 + f_2]$.
- **Open Simpson's 1/3 (n=2):** $I = (4h/3)[2f_1 - f_2 + 2f_3]$.
- **Open Simpson's 3/8 (n=3):** $I = (5h/24)[11f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + 11f_4]$.
- **Open Boole's (n=4):** $I = (3h/10)[11f_1 - 14f_2 + 26f_3 - 14f_4 + 11f_5]$.
- **Use open formulas** for improper integrals or when $f$ is singular/undefined at endpoints.
- Error order matches the polynomial degree: rule using degree-$n$ polynomial has error $\mathcal{O}(h^{n+2})$ (since $n+1$ derivatives appear in the error).
- For maximum accuracy, use **composite** open rules by dividing the interval into multiple panels.

### Common Mistake Matrix

| Mistake | Consequence | Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Using $h = (b-a)/n$ for open rule | Wrong node positions & weights | Use $h = (b-a)/(n+2)$ |
| Forgetting the 1's column in $X$ | $\beta_0$ absorbed into error | Always prepend a column of 1s |
| Confusing closed vs. open weights | Wrong answer | Verify formula against table |
| Reporting $R^2$ as if it were adjusted | Overstates model quality for many predictors | Use $R^2_{adj}$ for model selection |

### Recommended Study Order
1. Master **simple linear regression** first.
2. Understand the **column-of-1s** trick.
3. Practise at least **three** $3\times 3$ matrix inversions by hand.
4. Memorise the **error order** of each open Newton-Cotes rule.
5. Solve **at least 2 improper integral** problems using open rules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trapezoidal Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trapezoidal Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trapezoidal Rule (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Trapezoidal Rule** is a numerical integration technique used to approximate the definite integral $\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx$ by replacing the actual curve $y = f(x)$ over the interval $[a, b]$ with a **straight-line segment (chord)** joining the endpoints. The area enclosed between the curve and the $x$-axis is then approximated by the area of a **single trapezium** (simple rule) or by a series of **adjacent trapeziums** (composite rule).

**Mathematical Statement (Simple Trapezoidal Rule):**

$$
\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(a) + f(b)\bigr], \quad h = b - a
$$

**Mathematical Statement (Composite Trapezoidal Rule with $n$ subintervals):**

$$
\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_0) + 2\!\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\Bigr], \quad h = \frac{b - a}{n}
$$

where $x_i = a + i h$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect measuring the area of an oddly-shaped plot of land** with a wavy boundary (the curve $y = f(x)$). You cannot use the standard rectangle formula because the boundary is not flat. What do you do?

> **Real-World Analogy: "Connecting Two Posts with a Rope"**
> Suppose you place a **post at point $A$ (height $f(a)$)** and another **post at point $B$ (height $f(b)$)** on the boundary. Now tie a **tight rope** between the tops of these two posts. The rope becomes a **straight chord** cutting across the curve. The area beneath the curve is now approximated by the area of a **trapezium** ŌĆö a four-sided shape with two parallel sides ($f(a)$ and $f(b)$) and a width $h = b - a$.

If the curve is too wiggly and one trapezium gives a poor estimate, you can **add more posts at intermediate points** ($x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n-1}$) and connect them with **multiple ropes**, forming many small trapeziums side by side. The **more posts you plant, the closer the trapeziums hug the curve**, and the more accurate the area estimate becomes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Trapezoidal"?**
> In Euclidean geometry, a *trapezium* is a quadrilateral with **at least one pair of parallel sides**. In our case, the parallel sides are the vertical heights $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, and the horizontal base lies on the $x$-axis. Hence, the rule is called the Trapezoidal Rule.

### 1.3 Key Parameters & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **$h$ (step size):** The width of each subinterval. **Larger $h$ $\Rightarrow$ faster but coarser approximation.**
- **$n$ (number of subintervals):** The count of equal-width strips. **Larger $n$ $\Rightarrow$ higher accuracy, more computation.**
- **$O(h^2)$ accuracy:** The local truncation error per subinterval is of the **order of $h^2$**, making the rule a second-order accurate method.
- **Degree of precision: 1** ŌĆö exact for polynomials of degree $\leq 1$ (straight lines and constants).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö How the Rule Works (Step-by-Step)

The Trapezoidal Rule is built on three foundational pillars. Let us break them down:

**Step 1: Partition the Interval**
Divide the closed interval $[a, b]$ into $n$ equal subintervals of width

$$
h = \frac{b - a}{n}
$$

The **nodes** (partition points) are:

$$
x_i = a + i \cdot h, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Step 2: Approximate Each Panel with a Trapezium**
Over each subinterval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$, the curve $f(x)$ is replaced by the straight chord joining $(x_i, f(x_i))$ and $(x_{i+1}, f(x_{i+1}))$. The area under this chord in panel $i$ is:

$$
A_i = \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr]
$$

This is the standard geometric area formula for a trapezium: $\text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (\text{sum of parallel sides}) \times (\text{distance between them})$.

**Step 3: Sum All Trapeziums**
The total integral approximation is the algebraic sum of all panel areas:

$$
I \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} A_i = \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + 2f(x_1) + 2f(x_2) + \cdots + 2f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)\bigr]
$$

Notice the **coefficient pattern** ŌĆö interior points are multiplied by **2** (because each interior point is shared by two adjacent trapeziums), while the two endpoints are multiplied by **1**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Rule

- **Why a chord and not a rectangle?** A chord captures the *average height* between $f(x_i)$ and $f(x_{i+1})$ more faithfully than a rectangle (which uses only one endpoint's height, as in the Midpoint Rule). This averaging effect reduces error.
- **Why is the error $O(h^2)$?** Because the chord is a **first-degree polynomial approximation** of $f(x)$, and integration amplifies the interpolation error. The local error per panel scales as $h^3$, but dividing by the panel width $h$ gives a global per-panel error of $O(h^2)$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table** ŌĆö it contains every formula you need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on the Trapezoidal Rule.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Step size | $h = \dfrac{b - a}{n}$ | Width of each equal subinterval |
| 2 | Nodes | $x_i = a + i h,\; i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$ | Partition points including endpoints |
| 3 | Simple Trapezoidal Rule ($n=1$) | $I \approx \dfrac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + f(x_1)\bigr]$ | Single trapezium approximation |
| 4 | Composite Trapezoidal Rule | $I \approx \dfrac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_0) + 2\!\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\!f(x_i) + f(x_n)\Bigr]$ | Multiple trapeziums |
| 5 | Equivalent form | $I \approx \dfrac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + f(x_n)\bigr] + h\!\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\!f(x_i)$ | Useful when tabulating partial sums |
| 6 | Error (simple) | $E_T = -\dfrac{(b-a)^{3}}{12}\, f''(\xi)$ | Single-panel error, $\xi \in (a,b)$ |
| 7 | Error (composite) | $E_T = -\dfrac{(b-a)\,h^{2}}{12}\, f''(\mu)$ | Multi-panel error, $\mu \in (a,b)$ |
| 8 | Order of accuracy | $O(h^{2})$ | Global truncation error order |
| 9 | Degree of precision | 1 | Exact for polynomials of degree $\leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In valuation, the **sign of the error** is often ignored unless the question explicitly asks for the error term. Always state the **magnitude** as well as the direction (under- or over-estimates) for full marks.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The Trapezoidal Rule is widely deployed in engineering practice where analytical integration is impossible or impractical:

- **Civil Engineering:** Computing the **area of irregular cross-sections** of river channels, dams, and excavated earthwork profiles from surveyed field data.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Estimating **work done by a variable force** $W = \int F(x)\, dx$ from discrete force-displacement readings; computing **heat transfer** across non-uniform surfaces.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Approximating the **total charge** from a sampled current waveform $Q = \int i(t)\, dt$.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Evaluating **area under the curve (AUC)** in pharmacokinetics for drug concentration vs. time graphs.
- **Signal Processing:** Numerical integration of noisy sensor data when analytical antiderivatives are unavailable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Trapezoidal Rule

**Starting Premise:** We wish to evaluate

$$
I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 1:** Consider the two endpoint nodes $x_0 = a$ and $x_1 = b$ with corresponding function values $f(x_0)$ and $f(x_1)$. Let $h = x_1 - x_0 = b - a$.

**Step 2:** Construct the **first-degree Lagrange interpolating polynomial** $P_1(x)$ that passes exactly through $(x_0, f(x_0))$ and $(x_1, f(x_1))$:

$$
P_1(x) = f(x_0) \cdot \frac{x - x_1}{x_0 - x_1} \;+\; f(x_1) \cdot \frac{x - x_0}{x_1 - x_0}
$$

**Step 3:** Replace the true function $f(x)$ with its polynomial approximation $P_1(x)$ inside the integral:

$$
I = \int_{x_0}^{x_1} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \int_{x_0}^{x_1} P_1(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P_1(x)$ and integrate term by term. The denominator $x_0 - x_1 = -h$ and $x_1 - x_0 = h$:

$$
I \approx \int_{x_0}^{x_1} \!\left[\, f(x_0) \cdot \frac{x - x_1}{-h} \;+\; f(x_1) \cdot \frac{x - x_0}{h}\,\right] dx
$$

**Step 5:** Split the integral into two pieces:

$$
I \approx -\frac{f(x_0)}{h} \int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_1)\, dx \;+\; \frac{f(x_1)}{h} \int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_0)\, dx
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the two elementary integrals. Using $\int (x - c)\, dx = \frac{(x-c)^2}{2}$:

$$
\int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_1)\, dx = \left[\frac{(x - x_1)^2}{2}\right]_{x_0}^{x_1} = 0 - \frac{(x_0 - x_1)^2}{2} = -\frac{h^2}{2}
$$

$$
\int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_0)\, dx = \left[\frac{(x - x_0)^2}{2}\right]_{x_0}^{x_1} = \frac{(x_1 - x_0)^2}{2} - 0 = \frac{h^2}{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute these antiderivatives back into the equation:

$$
I \approx -\frac{f(x_0)}{h} \cdot \left(-\frac{h^2}{2}\right) \;+\; \frac{f(x_1)}{h} \cdot \left(\frac{h^2}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 8:** Simplify the algebra. The two $h$'s in numerator and denominator cancel:

$$
I \approx \frac{f(x_0) \cdot h}{2} \;+\; \frac{f(x_1) \cdot h}{2} \;=\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + f(x_1)\bigr]
$$

$$
\boxed{\;I \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(a) + f(b)\bigr]\;}
$$

This is the **Simple Trapezoidal Rule**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Composite Trapezoidal Rule

**Step 1:** Divide $[a, b]$ into $n$ subintervals of equal width $h = \frac{b - a}{n}$. The integral is split as a sum:

$$
\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx = \int_{x_0}^{x_1}\!f(x)\, dx + \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\!f(x)\, dx + \cdots + \int_{x_{n-1}}^{x_n}\!f(x)\, dx = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \int_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} f(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the simple Trapezoidal Rule to each subinterval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$:

$$
\int_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr], \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, n - 1
$$

**Step 3:** Sum all $n$ approximations:

$$
I \approx \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr] = \frac{h}{2} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \bigl[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr]
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the summation to expose the telescoping pattern:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \bigl[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})\bigr] = f(x_0) + 2f(x_1) + 2f(x_2) + \cdots + 2f(x_{n-1}) + f(x_n)
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that every interior node $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{n-1}$ appears **exactly twice** (once as the right end of one panel, once as the left end of the next), while the two boundary nodes $x_0$ and $x_n$ each appear **exactly once**.

**Step 6:** Therefore, the composite rule is:

$$
\boxed{\;I \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\Bigl[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\Bigr]\;}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Truncation Error

**Step 1:** The exact error in a single panel is:

$$
E_T = \int_{x_0}^{x_1} f(x)\, dx - \int_{x_0}^{x_1} P_1(x)\, dx = \int_{x_0}^{x_1} \bigl[f(x) - P_1(x)\bigr]\, dx
$$

**Step 2:** Recall the Lagrange remainder for first-degree interpolation:

$$
f(x) - P_1(x) = \frac{f''(\xi)}{2!}\,(x - x_0)(x - x_1)
$$

where $\xi$ depends on $x$ and lies in the open interval $(x_0, x_1)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the error integral:

$$
E_T = \int_{x_0}^{x_1} \frac{f''(\xi)}{2}\,(x - x_0)(x - x_1)\, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Since $f''$ is continuous on $[x_0, x_1]$, apply the **Mean Value Theorem for Integrals** ŌĆö there exists some $\eta \in (x_0, x_1)$ such that:

$$
E_T = \frac{f''(\eta)}{2} \int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_0)(x - x_1)\, dx
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral by expanding $(x - x_0)(x - x_1) = x^2 - (x_0 + x_1)x + x_0 x_1$:

$$
\int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_0)(x - x_1)\, dx = \left[\frac{x^3}{3} - \frac{(x_0 + x_1)x^2}{2} + x_0 x_1\, x\right]_{x_0}^{x_1}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the limits. After algebraic simplification (the cubic terms cancel symmetrically), the result is:

$$
\int_{x_0}^{x_1} (x - x_0)(x - x_1)\, dx = -\frac{h^3}{6}
$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$
E_T = \frac{f''(\eta)}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{h^3}{6}\right) = -\frac{h^3}{12}\, f''(\eta)
$$

**Step 8:** Recalling $h = b - a$ for the simple rule, the error formula is:

$$
\boxed{\;E_T = -\frac{(b - a)^{3}}{12}\, f''(\eta), \quad \eta \in (a, b)\;}
$$

**Step 9:** For the **composite rule** with $n$ panels, sum the individual errors. Each panel has width $h = \frac{b-a}{n}$, and using a global mean value $\mu \in (a, b)$:

$$
E_T^{\text{comp}} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \left(-\frac{h^3}{12}\right) f''(\eta_i) = -\frac{n h^3}{12} f''(\mu) = -\frac{(b - a) h^{2}}{12} f''(\mu)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;E_T^{\text{comp}} = -\frac{(b - a)\, h^{2}}{12}\, f''(\mu)\;}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Worked Examples

#### **Example 1: Simple Trapezoidal Rule (n = 1)**

Evaluate $\displaystyle I = \int_{0}^{1} x^{2}\, dx$ using the simple Trapezoidal Rule. Compare with the exact value $I = \tfrac{1}{3}$.

**Solution:**

Identify $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $f(x) = x^2$, $h = 1$.

$$
f(a) = f(0) = 0, \quad f(b) = f(1) = 1
$$

Apply the simple rule:

$$
I \approx \frac{h}{2}[f(0) + f(1)] = \frac{1}{2}[0 + 1] = 0.5
$$

**Absolute Error:** $|0.5 - 0.333\ldots| = 0.1667$

The rule **overestimates** the area because the chord lies above the concave-up parabola.

---

#### **Example 2: Composite Trapezoidal Rule (n = 4)**

Evaluate $\displaystyle I = \int_{0}^{2} e^{x}\, dx$ using the composite Trapezoidal Rule with $n = 4$.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the step size:

$$
h = \frac{b - a}{n} = \frac{2 - 0}{4} = 0.5
$$

Step 2: Generate the nodes and function values:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $f(x_i) = e^{x_i}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.0 | 1.0000 |
| 1 | 0.5 | 1.6487 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 2.7183 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 4.4817 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 7.3891 |

Step 3: Apply the composite rule:

$$
I \approx \frac{h}{2}\left[f(x_0) + 2\bigl(f(x_1) + f(x_2) + f(x_3)\bigr) + f(x_4)\right]
$$

Substitute the tabulated values:

$$
I \approx \frac{0.5}{2}\bigl[1.0000 + 2(1.6487 + 2.7183 + 4.4817) + 7.3891\bigr]
$$

$$
= 0.25 \times \bigl[1.0000 + 2(8.8487) + 7.3891\bigr]
$$

$$
= 0.25 \times \bigl[1.0000 + 17.6974 + 7.3891\bigr] = 0.25 \times 26.0865 = 6.5216
$$

**Exact value:** $I = e^2 - e^0 = 7.3891 - 1.0000 = 6.3891$

**Absolute Error:** $|6.5216 - 6.3891| = 0.1325$

The rule **overestimates** because the exponential function is convex (concave up) over $[0, 2]$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Geometric Interpretation ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of how the Trapezoidal Rule maps the continuous integration problem into a discrete summation. It also visualizes the geometric relationship between the chord, the trapezium, and the true curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Define f of x, a, b, n"] --> B["COMPUTE: h equals b minus a divided by n"]
    B --> C["GENERATE NODES: x sub i equals a plus i times h"]
    C --> D["EVALUATE: f at each x sub i"]
    D --> E["APPLY RULE: I approx h over 2 times f at x0 plus 2 times sum f at x sub i plus f at x sub n"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT: Approximate integral value I"]
    D --> G["SIDE ANALYSIS: Compute f double prime of mu for error estimation"]
    G --> H["ERROR: E sub T equals negative h squared times b minus a times f double prime of mu over 12"]
    F --> I["DECISION: Is tolerance met"]
    I -->|NO| J["INCREMENT n and repeat"]
    I -->|YES| K["FINAL ANSWER: Converged I"]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart ŌĆö Partitioning Strategy

This second diagram zooms into the **partitioning and panel-by-panel trapezium construction** step, which is the conceptual heart of the composite rule.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["PANEL 0: x0 to x1"] --> S0["Area equals h over 2 times f of x0 plus f of x1"]
    P1["PANEL 1: x1 to x2"] --> S1["Area equals h over 2 times f of x1 plus f of x2"]
    P2["PANEL 2: x2 to x3"] --> S2["Area equals h over 2 times f of x2 plus f of x3"]
    Pn["PANEL n minus 1: x sub n minus 1 to x sub n"] --> Sn["Area equals h over 2 times f of x sub n minus 1 plus f of x sub n"]
    S0 --> SUM["SUM ALL PANEL AREAS"]
    S1 --> SUM
    S2 --> SUM
    Sn --> SUM
    SUM --> COEFF["INTERIOR COEFFICIENT 2 SHARED BY ADJACENT PANELS"]
    COEFF --> RESULT["RESULT: I approx h over 2 bracket f x0 plus 2 sum f xi plus f xn bracket"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping ŌĆö Curve to Trapeziums

This diagram maps the geometric intuition of replacing the smooth curve with discrete trapeziums:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["TRUE CURVE: y equals f of x over interval a to b"] --> M1["OBSERVATION: f is unknown analytically OR anti-derivative intractable"]
    M1 --> M2["STRATEGY: Replace curve by n straight chords connecting nodes"]
    M2 --> M3["RESULT: n adjacent trapeziums approximate the area"]
    M3 --> M4["MATHEMATICAL MODEL: Each trapezium area equals h times average height"]
    M4 --> M5["SUMMATION: Total area equals h over 2 bracket endpoint sum plus 2 times interior sum bracket"]
    M5 --> M6["REMAINING GAP: Truncation error of order h squared"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The three diagrams above together form a **three-layer understanding**:
> 1. The **first flowchart** answers *"How do I run the algorithm?"*
> 2. The **second flowchart** answers *"Why does the interior coefficient become 2?"*
> 3. The **third flowchart** answers *"What is the conceptual justification for this approximation?"*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. State the Composite Trapezoidal Rule for numerical integration. Mention its geometric interpretation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Composite Trapezoidal Rule with $n$ equal subintervals of width $h = \frac{b-a}{n}$ is given by:

$$
\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{2}\left[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\right]
$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The curve $y = f(x)$ between $x = a$ and $x = b$ is approximated by **$n$ straight-line chords** joining successive nodes $(x_i, f(x_i))$ and $(x_{i+1}, f(x_{i+1}))$. The region between the curve and the $x$-axis is partitioned into **$n$ trapeziums**, each of area $\frac{h}{2}[f(x_i) + f(x_{i+1})]$. Summing all $n$ trapezium areas yields the integral approximation. Interior points receive a coefficient of **2** because each is shared by two neighbouring trapeziums.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**Q2. Derive the order of accuracy of the Trapezoidal Rule using its error term.**

**Model Answer:**

The truncation error of the composite Trapezoidal Rule with step size $h$ is:

$$
E_T = -\frac{(b - a)\, h^{2}}{12}\, f''(\mu), \quad \mu \in (a, b)
$$

Since the error is **directly proportional to $h^2$**, the rule is said to be of **order 2**, denoted $O(h^2)$. As $h \to 0$ (equivalently, $n \to \infty$), the error decreases as the **square of the step size**, meaning that halving $h$ reduces the error by a factor of approximately 4.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Using the **Composite Trapezoidal Rule** with $n = 6$ subintervals, evaluate the integral

$$
I = \int_{1}^{4} \frac{1}{x}\, dx
$$

and estimate the truncation error. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the exact value of the integral is $\ln 4$, find the **actual error** and verify it against the **estimated error**. Compute the **percentage relative error**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $a = 1$, $b = 4$, $n = 6$, $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$.

**Step 2:** Step size:

$$
h = \frac{b - a}{n} = \frac{4 - 1}{6} = 0.5
$$

**Step 3:** Nodes and function values:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $f(x_i) = 1/x_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1.0 | 1.000000 |
| 1 | 1.5 | 0.666667 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 0.500000 |
| 3 | 2.5 | 0.400000 |
| 4 | 3.0 | 0.333333 |
| 5 | 3.5 | 0.285714 |
| 6 | 4.0 | 0.250000 |

**Step 4:** Apply the composite rule:

$$
I \approx \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + 2\bigl(f(x_1) + f(x_2) + f(x_3) + f(x_4) + f(x_5)\bigr) + f(x_6)\bigr]
$$

Sum the interior values:

$$
S_{\text{int}} = 0.666667 + 0.500000 + 0.400000 + 0.333333 + 0.285714 = 2.185714
$$

Substitute:

$$
I \approx \frac{0.5}{2}\bigl[1.000000 + 2(2.185714) + 0.250000\bigr] = 0.25 \times [1.000000 + 4.371428 + 0.250000]
$$

$$
= 0.25 \times 5.621428 = 1.405357
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{approx}} \approx 1.4054}
$$

**Step 5:** Estimate the truncation error. Compute $f''(x)$:

$$
f(x) = x^{-1}, \quad f'(x) = -x^{-2}, \quad f''(x) = 2x^{-3} = \frac{2}{x^{3}}
$$

Since $f''(x) = \frac{2}{x^3}$ is **decreasing** on $[1, 4]$, its maximum magnitude occurs at $x = 1$:

$$
\max \vert f''(x) \vert = f''(1) = 2
$$

The error bound is:

$$
\vert E_T \vert \leq \frac{(b - a)\, h^{2}}{12} \max_{\mu \in [1,4]} \vert f''(\mu) \vert = \frac{3 \times 0.25}{12} \times 2 = \frac{0.75}{12} \times 2 = 0.125
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert E_T \vert \leq 0.125}
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Exact value:

$$
I_{\text{exact}} = \ln 4 = 1.386294
$$

**Step 2:** Actual (true) error:

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = I_{\text{exact}} - I_{\text{approx}} = 1.386294 - 1.405357 = -0.019063
$$

The **negative sign** confirms the rule **overestimates** the integral (since $f(x) = 1/x$ is convex on $[1, 4]$, the chord lies above the curve).

**Step 3:** Magnitude of actual error:

$$
\vert E_{\text{actual}} \vert = 0.019063
$$

**Step 4:** Verify against the estimated bound. The actual error $0.019063$ is indeed **less than** the upper bound $0.125$, confirming the error formula is valid (it is a conservative bound).

**Step 5:** Percentage relative error:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert E_{\text{actual}} \vert}{I_{\text{exact}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.019063}{1.386294} \times 100 \approx 1.375\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Percentage Relative Error} \approx 1.375\%}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Evaluate $\displaystyle \int_{0}^{1} \frac{dx}{1 + x^{2}}$ using the **Composite Trapezoidal Rule** with $n = 8$ subintervals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The exact value of the integral is $\frac{\pi}{4}$. Determine the **true error** and the **percentage error**. Comment on whether increasing $n$ would improve the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Parameters: $a = 0$, $b = 1$, $n = 8$, $f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + x^2}$.

**Step 2:** Step size:

$$
h = \frac{1 - 0}{8} = 0.125
$$

**Step 3:** Nodes and function values:

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $f(x_i) = \frac{1}{1 + x_i^2}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.00000 | 1.000000 |
| 1 | 0.125 | 0.01563 | 0.984615 |
| 2 | 0.250 | 0.06250 | 0.941176 |
| 3 | 0.375 | 0.14063 | 0.876712 |
| 4 | 0.500 | 0.25000 | 0.800000 |
| 5 | 0.625 | 0.39063 | 0.719101 |
| 6 | 0.750 | 0.56250 | 0.640000 |
| 7 | 0.875 | 0.76563 | 0.566372 |
| 8 | 1.000 | 1.00000 | 0.500000 |

**Step 4:** Sum the interior values $f(x_1)$ through $f(x_7)$:

$$
S_{\text{int}} = 0.984615 + 0.941176 + 0.876712 + 0.800000 + 0.719101 + 0.640000 + 0.566372
$$

$$
S_{\text{int}} = 5.527976
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the composite rule:

$$
I \approx \frac{0.125}{2}\bigl[1.000000 + 2(5.527976) + 0.500000\bigr]
$$

$$
= 0.0625 \times \bigl[1.000000 + 11.055952 + 0.500000\bigr]
$$

$$
= 0.0625 \times 12.555952 = 0.784747
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{approx}} \approx 0.7847}
$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Exact value:

$$
I_{\text{exact}} = \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3.141593}{4} = 0.785398
$$

**Step 2:** True error:

$$
E_{\text{true}} = I_{\text{exact}} - I_{\text{approx}} = 0.785398 - 0.784747 = 0.000651
$$

The **positive sign** indicates the rule **underestimates** the integral here because the chord cuts below the concave-down portion of the curve near the endpoints.

**Step 3:** Percentage error:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{0.000651}{0.785398} \times 100 \approx 0.0829\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Percentage Error} \approx 0.083\%}
$$

**Step 4:** **Discussion on increasing $n$:** Since the Composite Trapezoidal Rule has $O(h^2)$ convergence, doubling $n$ (i.e., halving $h$) reduces the error by a factor of approximately **4**. For instance, if we doubled to $n = 16$, we would expect the error to drop to roughly $0.000651/4 \approx 0.00016$, giving an error of about **0.02%**. This rapid convergence makes the rule efficient for smooth functions like $\frac{1}{1+x^2}$, though for very oscillatory integrands, other methods like **Simpson's Rule** ($O(h^4)$) or **Gaussian Quadrature** may be more appropriate.

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the coefficient 2 on interior nodes.** Students frequently write $\frac{h}{2}[f(x_0) + f(x_1) + \cdots + f(x_n)]$ without doubling the interior terms. This error alone costs **3ŌĆō4 marks** in a 14-mark question. **Always remember: endpoints ├Ś 1, interior ├Ś 2.**
>
> 2. **Miscomputing $h$.** A very common slip is $h = \frac{b - a}{n - 1}$ instead of $\frac{b - a}{n}$. This single mistake propagates through all subsequent calculations and is a **mass-failure trigger**. Double-check: $h = \frac{b-a}{n}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing the sign of error with the sign of deviation.** A negative $E_T$ means the approximation is **larger** than the exact value (overestimate), not the other way around. Articulating this correctly fetches the **interpretation mark**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the error formulas for simple vs. composite rules.** The simple rule error is $-\frac{(b-a)^3}{12} f''(\xi)$ ŌĆö note the **cube of $h$**. The composite rule error is $-\frac{(b-a) h^2}{12} f''(\mu)$ ŌĆö note the **square of $h$** multiplied by $(b-a)$. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Omitting the table of values.** KTU valuation scripts expect a **clearly tabulated** set of $x_i$ and $f(x_i)$ values before the substitution step. Skipping this presentation costs **1ŌĆō2 marks** for *lack of structured working*.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Trapezoidal Rule**

- **Simple Trapezoidal Rule:** $I \approx \frac{h}{2}[f(a) + f(b)]$ with $h = b - a$.
- **Composite Trapezoidal Rule:** $I \approx \frac{h}{2}\bigl[f(x_0) + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} f(x_i) + f(x_n)\bigr]$ with $h = \frac{b-a}{n}$.
- **Coefficient Rule:** Endpoints get **├Ś1**, interior nodes get **├Ś2** (shared by two adjacent panels).
- **Error ŌĆö Simple:** $E_T = -\frac{(b-a)^3}{12} f''(\xi)$, where $\xi \in (a, b)$.
- **Error ŌĆö Composite:** $E_T = -\frac{(b-a) h^2}{12} f''(\mu)$, where $\mu \in (a, b)$.
- **Order of accuracy:** $O(h^2)$ ŌĆö halving $h$ reduces error by a factor of **4**.
- **Degree of precision:** 1 ŌĆö the rule is **exact** for polynomials of degree $\leq 1$.
- **Sign convention:** Negative $E_T \Rightarrow$ **overestimate** (chord above curve, e.g., convex functions).
- **Applications:** Irregular land areas, work done by variable forces, AUC in pharmacokinetics, total charge from current waveforms, heat transfer integration.
- **When to avoid:** Functions with rapid oscillations, singularities, or sharp peaks ŌĆö use **Simpson's Rule** ($O(h^4)$) or **Gaussian Quadrature** for those.
- **KTU-Specific Mnemonic:** *"**T**rapezoidal = **T**wo parallel sides + **T**wo-slope averaging."*
- **Most-tested sub-question patterns in KTU ESE:**
  1. Direct numerical evaluation with $n = 4, 6, 8$.
  2. Error estimation given $f''(x)$.
  3. Comparison of actual vs. estimated error and percentage error.
  4. Proof/derivation of the formula or order of accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simpson's Rules (Review)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simpson's Rules (Review)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Simpson's Rules** are a family of closed NewtonŌĆōCotes quadrature formulas used in numerical integration to approximate the definite integral of a function $f(x)$ over a finite interval $[a, b]$ by fitting low-order polynomials (parabolas or cubics) through equally-spaced sample points.

There are two principal variants under the KTU 2024 PECET417 Module 2 syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simpson's 1/3 Rule (Simple Form):** Approximates $\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx$ by fitting a single second-degree parabola through **three equally spaced ordinates** $y_0, y_1, y_2$ separated by step size $h$.
>
> $$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\,\big[\,y_0 + 4y_1 + y_2\,\big]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simpson's 3/8 Rule (Simple Form):** Approximates $\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx$ by fitting a single third-degree cubic through **four equally spaced ordinates** $y_0, y_1, y_2, y_3$ separated by step size $h$.
>
> $$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{3h}{8}\,\big[\,y_0 + 3y_1 + 3y_2 + y_3\,\big]$$

Both are extended to the **Composite Form** when the interval is divided into multiple sub-intervals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "Connecting the Dots with Curves":**
> Imagine you are an architect asked to estimate the total floor area of a curved landscape between two walls. You cannot measure every point, so you take elevation readings at equal vertical strips.
>
> - The **Trapezoidal Rule** is like connecting the top of each strip with a straight ruler ŌĆö a flat roof. It is rough but never overshoots wildly.
> - **Simpson's 1/3 Rule** is like asking a carpenter to bend a flexible metal strip (a parabola) so it passes through three consecutive points. The bend captures the gentle curvature of the landscape much better.
> - **Simpson's 3/8 Rule** is even fancier ŌĆö a *cubic* strip (with an inflection) that fits four consecutive points, excellent for highly wavy terrain.

In short: **higher-order polynomial fit ŌćÆ better accuracy ŌćÆ fewer sample points needed**, but at the cost of stricter spacing rules.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary and Physical Metrics

- **Step size** ($h$): the uniform distance between consecutive $x$-values. **Units: same as $x$**.
- **Sub-interval** ($n$): number of equal segments into which $[a, b]$ is divided.
- **Ordinate** ($y_i$): the function value $f(x_i)$ at sample point $x_i$.
- **Degree of accuracy**: the highest-degree polynomial for which the rule gives an *exact* result. For Simpson's 1/3 it is **3**; for 3/8 it is also **3**.
- **Order of error**: the leading power of $h$ in the truncation error term.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "1/3" and "3/8"?**
> The coefficients $\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{3}{8}$ come directly from integrating the Lagrange-interpolated polynomial over a unit step. They are not arbitrarily chosen ŌĆö they are mathematically optimal weights that make the rule exact for polynomials up to a given degree.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" ŌĆö Why Fit Polynomials at All?

Numerical integration works because:

1. We can always evaluate $f(x)$ at chosen points $x_i$.
2. Any smooth curve can be *locally* approximated by a polynomial.
3. Polynomials have known, easy antiderivatives.

The **closed NewtonŌĆōCotes** family (Trapezoidal, Simpson's 1/3, Simpson's 3/8, Boole's, etc.) is built by integrating Lagrange-interpolating polynomials that pass through equally spaced data.

## 2.2 Operational Steps ŌĆö How Simpson's 1/3 Works

**Step A ŌĆö Partition the interval.** Divide $[a, b]$ into $n$ **even** number of sub-intervals. **Simpson's 1/3 requires $n$ to be even** because each parabolic patch covers *two* sub-intervals.

$$h = \frac{b - a}{n}, \qquad x_i = a + i h, \qquad y_i = f(x_i)$$

**Step B ŌĆö Group points in pairs.** Each parabolic patch uses three consecutive ordinates, so we apply the simple rule to every pair of sub-intervals.

**Step C ŌĆö Apply the composite formula.**

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{h}{3}\,\Big[\,(y_0 + y_n) + 4\,(y_1 + y_3 + \cdots + y_{n-1}) + 2\,(y_2 + y_4 + \cdots + y_{n-2})\,\Big]$$

**Step D ŌĆö Estimate error.** Compare with Richardson's extrapolation, halve $h$, or use the analytical error bound.

## 2.3 Operational Steps ŌĆö How Simpson's 3/8 Works

**Step A ŌĆö Partition the interval.** Divide $[a, b]$ into $n$ sub-intervals where $n$ is a **multiple of 3**, because each cubic patch covers *three* sub-intervals.

**Step B ŌĆö Group points in triples.** Each cubic patch uses four consecutive ordinates.

**Step C ŌĆö Apply the composite formula.**

$$\int_{a}^{b} f(x)\,dx \;\approx\; \frac{3h}{8}\,\Big[\,(y_0 + y_n) + 3\,(y_1 + y_2 + y_4 + y_5 + \cdots) + 2\,(y_3 + y_6 + \cdots)\Big]$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the single most important revision block for this topic. Memorize it before any numerical test.

| # | Rule | Interval Count | Composite Formula | Order of Error | Error Term (Single Patch) |
|---|------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Trapezoidal** | $n \geq 1$ | $\dfrac{h}{2}\big[(y_0 + y_n) + 2(y_1 + y_2 + \cdots + y_{n-1})\big]$ | $O(h^2)$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)}{12}\,h^2\,f''(\xi)$ |
| 2 | **Simpson's 1/3** | $n$ **even** | $\dfrac{h}{3}\big[(y_0 + y_n) + 4\sum y_{odd} + 2\sum y_{even\,(internal)}\big]$ | $O(h^4)$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)}{180}\,h^4\,f^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| 3 | **Simpson's 3/8** | $n$ **multiple of 3** | $\dfrac{3h}{8}\big[(y_0 + y_n) + 3\sum y_{(not\,multiple\,3)} + 2\sum y_{(multiples\,3,\,internal)}\big]$ | $O(h^4)$ | $-\dfrac{(b-a)}{80}\,h^4\,f^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| 4 | **Simple 1/3 (n=2)** | 2 sub-ints | $\dfrac{h}{3}[y_0 + 4y_1 + y_2]$ | $O(h^5)$ | $-\dfrac{h^5}{90}\,f^{(4)}(\xi)$ |
| 5 | **Simple 3/8 (n=3)** | 3 sub-ints | $\dfrac{3h}{8}[y_0 + 3y_1 + 3y_2 + y_3]$ | $O(h^5)$ | $-\dfrac{3h^5}{80}\,f^{(4)}(\xi)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the multipliers in the composite formulas:
> - **4** for Simpson's 1/3 ŌåÆ applied to all odd-indexed ordinates.
> - **2** for Simpson's 1/3 ŌåÆ applied to all internal even-indexed ordinates (excluding endpoints).
> - **3** for Simpson's 3/8 ŌåÆ applied to ordinates whose index is **not** a multiple of 3.
> - **2** for Simpson's 3/8 ŌåÆ applied to ordinates whose index **is** a multiple of 3 (internal only).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Simpson's rules are foundational in:
> - **Civil Engineering:** Computing area of irregular land plots from surveyed offsets, or volume of earthwork from contour data.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Estimating work done by variable force $W = \int F\,dx$ from tabulated force readings.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Computing total charge from a sampled current waveform $q = \int i(t)\,dt$.
> - **Computer Graphics:** Approximating arc lengths and B├®zier curve areas.
> - **Data Science:** Numerical evaluation of definite integrals where no closed-form antiderivative exists (e.g., error function, $\sin(x)/x$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Simple Simpson's 1/3 Rule

**Goal:** Derive $\int_{x_0}^{x_2} f(x)\,dx \approx \frac{h}{3}\,[y_0 + 4y_1 + y_2]$ from first principles.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the geometry

We have three equally-spaced abscissas $x_0, x_1, x_2$ with common spacing $h$:
