$$E_4 = y_{\text{exact}}(0.4) - y_4^{\text{Euler}} = 1.58365 - 1.5282 = 0.05545$$

**Relative Percentage Error:**

$$\text{Error \%} = \frac{0.05545}{1.58365} \times 100 \approx 3.50\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU Students:** Notice the error grows monotonically. This is characteristic of Euler's method because each step *underestimates* the curvature contribution. To improve accuracy, students can halve the step size to $h = 0.05$ and recompute ŌĆö the error will drop by approximately a factor of 2 (linear convergence).

---

## 3.3 Error Order Derivation (Local Truncation Error)

The local truncation error at step $n$ is the difference between the exact solution update and the Euler update, assuming $y_n$ is exact:

$$\tau_{n+1} = y(x_{n+1}) - \left[y(x_n) + h \cdot f(x_n, y(x_n))\right]$$

Expanding using Taylor's theorem with remainder:

$$y(x_{n+1}) = y(x_n) + h \cdot f(x_n, y(x_n)) + \frac{h^2}{2!} \cdot y''(\xi_n), \quad \xi_n \in (x_n, x_{n+1})$$

Subtracting:

$$\tau_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2} \cdot y''(\xi_n) = O(h^2)$$

**Textual Explanation:** The local truncation error at each step is proportional to $h^2$. Since we take $n \propto \frac{1}{h}$ steps, the **global error** accumulates to:

$$E_{\text{global}} = n \cdot O(h^2) = O(h)$$

This proves that Euler's method is a **first-order accurate** method.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Algorithmic Flow of Euler's Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Input ODE] --> B[Extract f of x and y from dy/dx]
    B --> C[Read Initial Condition x0, y0]
    C --> D[Read Step Size h and Target X]
    D --> E[Compute n = X minus x0 divided by h]
    E --> F[Initialize i = 0]
    F --> G{Decision: i less than n}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Slope m = f at xi, yi]
    H --> I[Update y new = yi + h times m]
    I --> J[Update x new = xi + h]
    J --> K[Set yi = y new and xi = x new]
    K --> L[Increment i = i + 1]
    L --> G
    G -- No --> M[Output Final Value yn at X]
    M --> N[END]
```

**Textual Walkthrough of the Diagram:**

- The algorithm begins by **parsing the ODE** to extract the slope function $f(x, y)$.
- The **initial condition** and the **discretization parameters** ($h$, $X$) are loaded.
- A loop is entered that runs exactly $n$ times.
- At every iteration, the slope is evaluated at the *current* approximate point, and a **forward linear extrapolation** is performed.
- After the loop terminates, the final value $y_n$ is returned as the numerical approximation to $y(X)$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of the Numerical Solver Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Layer: ODE and IC] --> B[Processing Layer: Euler Engine]
    B --> C[Output Layer: Tabulated Solution]
    A1[Function f of x and y] --> B
    A2[Initial Point x0, y0] --> B
    A3[Step Size h] --> B
    A4[Target X] --> B
    B --> B1[Sub-block 1: Slope Evaluator]
    B --> B2[Sub-block 2: State Updater]
    B --> B3[Sub-block 3: Iterator Controller]
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B1
    C --> C1[Table of xi and yi]
    C --> C2[Graphical Plot Comparison]
    C --> C3[Error Metrics]
```

**Textual Walkthrough of the Architecture:**

- The **Input Layer** packages all problem data: the slope function, the initial condition, the step size, and the target.
- The **Processing Layer** is the Euler engine, which contains three interacting sub-blocks:
  - The **Slope Evaluator** computes $f(x_i, y_i)$.
  - The **State Updater** applies the recursion $y_{i+1} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i)$.
  - The **Iterator Controller** advances the index and checks the termination condition.
- The **Output Layer** produces the final deliverables: a tabulation of $(x_i, y_i)$, a plot overlay against the true solution, and error metrics for analysis.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram ŌĆö Step-by-Step Iteration Trace

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Controller
    participant Slope as Slope Evaluator
    participant Updater as State Updater
    Main ->> Slope: Request slope at x0 and y0
    Slope -->> Main: Return m0 equals f of x0 and y0
    Main ->> Updater: Apply y1 equals y0 plus h times m0
    Updater -->> Main: Return y1
    Main ->> Main: Advance x1 equals x0 plus h
    Main ->> Slope: Request slope at x1 and y1
    Slope -->> Main: Return m1 equals f of x1 and y1
    Main ->> Updater: Apply y2 equals y1 plus h times m1
    Updater -->> Main: Return y2
    Main ->> Main: Advance x2 equals x1 plus h
    Main ->> Main: Continue until target X is reached
    Main ->> Main: Output final yn
```

**Textual Walkthrough:** This sequence diagram visualizes the *temporal interaction* between the three core sub-blocks during a single Euler iteration. The cycle repeats for every step until the target $X$ is reached, producing the final numerical approximation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Euler's Method for solving an initial value problem. State its order of accuracy and write the truncation error.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Euler's Method is an explicit one-step numerical procedure for solving a first-order initial value problem of the form

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y), \quad y(x_0) = y_0$$

The recurrence relation is

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$$

**Order of accuracy:** It is a **first-order method**, meaning the global error is $O(h)$.

**Local truncation error:** $\tau_{n+1} = \dfrac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n)$, which is $O(h^2)$.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]

**Q: Why is Euler's Method classified as a first-order method? Mention one practical limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Euler's Method is obtained by truncating the Taylor series expansion of $y(x_{n+1})$ about $x_n$ after the linear $h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ term. All terms of order $h^2$ and higher are dropped, making the local truncation error proportional to $h^2$. Since the number of steps $n \propto \frac{1}{h}$, the global error is $O(h)$, which is the defining property of a first-order method.

**Limitation:** For small step sizes, Euler's method requires a very large number of iterations to reach a distant target point, leading to **accumulated round-off and truncation errors**, making it unsuitable for long-range integration.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and are split into two sub-parts, usually **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below, two full alternative questions are provided for practice.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

Using Euler's Method, find the approximate value of $y$ at $x = 0.2$ for the differential equation

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y$$

with $y(0) = 1$, taking step size $h = 0.1$. Tabulate the iterations.

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Inputs:**

$f(x, y) = x^2 + y$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$, target $X = 0.2$.

Number of steps: $n = \frac{0.2 - 0}{0.1} = 2$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Iteration 1 ($i = 0$ to $i = 1$):**

Slope at initial point:

$$f(x_0, y_0) = f(0, 1) = (0)^2 + 1 = 1$$

Euler update:

$$y_1 = y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 1 + (0.1)(1) = 1.1$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Iteration 2 ($i = 1$ to $i = 2$):**

Slope at $x_1 = 0.1, y_1 = 1.1$:

$$f(x_1, y_1) = (0.1)^2 + 1.1 = 0.01 + 1.1 = 1.11$$

Euler update:

$$y_2 = y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) = 1.1 + (0.1)(1.11) = 1.1 + 0.111 = 1.211$$

**Result:** $y(0.2) \approx \mathbf{1.211}$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

For the same IVP in part (a), the **exact solution** is

$$y(x) = -x^2 - 2x - 2 + 4 e^{x}$$

Compute the **true error** and **relative percentage error** at $x = 0.2$. Also comment on how the error would change if the step size were reduced to $h = 0.05$.

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Exact Value at $x = 0.2$:**

$$y_{\text{exact}}(0.2) = -(0.2)^2 - 2(0.2) - 2 + 4 e^{0.2}$$

$$= -0.04 - 0.4 - 2 + 4(1.22140)$$

$$= -2.44 + 4.88562 = 2.44562$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö True Error:**

$$E_2 = y_{\text{exact}}(0.2) - y_2^{\text{Euler}} = 2.44562 - 1.211 = 1.23462$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Relative Percentage Error:**

$$\text{Error \%} = \frac{1.23462}{2.44562} \times 100 \approx 50.49\%$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comment on Halving $h$:**

Since Euler's Method is first-order, halving $h$ will approximately halve the global error. If $h = 0.05$, we would take 4 steps instead of 2, and the true error would reduce from $1.23462$ to approximately $0.617$. To achieve a relative error below $1\%$, one would need to take the step size down to $h \approx 0.005$, requiring 40 iterations ŌĆö illustrating the inefficiency of Euler's method for high-accuracy requirements.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model Paper]

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks

Using Euler's Method, solve the initial value problem

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = -2xy^2, \quad y(0) = 1$$

to find $y$ at $x = 0.3$ with step size $h = 0.1$. Present results in a neat table.

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Inputs:** $f(x, y) = -2xy^2$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$, $X = 0.3$.

Number of steps: $n = 3$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$f(0, 1) = -2(0)(1)^2 = 0$$

$$y_1 = 1 + (0.1)(0) = 1.0000$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$f(0.1, 1.0000) = -2(0.1)(1.0000)^2 = -0.2$$

$$y_2 = 1.0000 + (0.1)(-0.2) = 0.9800$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$f(0.2, 0.9800) = -2(0.2)(0.9800)^2 = -0.4 \times 0.9604 = -0.38416$$

$$y_3 = 0.9800 + (0.1)(-0.38416) = 0.9800 - 0.038416 = 0.941584$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| Step $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $f(x_i, y_i)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 1.000000 | 0.000000 |
| 1 | 0.1 | 1.000000 | -0.200000 |
| 2 | 0.2 | 0.980000 | -0.384160 |
| 3 | 0.3 | 0.941584 | ŌĆö |

**Final Answer:** $y(0.3) \approx \mathbf{0.9416}$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks

The exact solution to the IVP in part (a) is given by $y(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + x^2}$. Compute the true error and relative percentage error at $x = 0.3$. Also explain whether Euler's Method overestimates or underestimates the true solution for this problem.

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Exact Value at $x = 0.3$:**

$$y_{\text{exact}}(0.3) = \frac{1}{1 + (0.3)^2} = \frac{1}{1.09} \approx 0.917431$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö True Error:**

$$E_3 = y_{\text{exact}}(0.3) - y_3^{\text{Euler}} = 0.917431 - 0.941584 = -0.024153$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Relative Percentage Error:**

$$\text{Error \%} = \frac{\vert -0.024153 \vert}{0.917431} \times 100 \approx 2.63\%$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Overestimation vs. Underestimation:**

Since the true error is **negative** ($y_{\text{Euler}} > y_{\text{exact}}$), Euler's Method **overestimates** the true solution in this case. Geometrically, this is because the true solution $y = \frac{1}{1 + x^2}$ is **concave up** (second derivative positive) near the origin, meaning the linear Euler segments lie *above* the true curve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Failure to update the independent variable:** Students often forget to recompute $x_{i+1} = x_i + h$ and instead reuse $x_i$ in the next slope evaluation. This silently corrupts every subsequent step. **(ŌłÆ2 marks)**
> 2. **Substituting approximate $y_n$ vs. exact $y(x_n)$:** When asked for the *local* truncation error, students must use $y(x_n)$, the true value. When computing actual iterates, they must use the approximate $y_n$. Mixing these loses **(ŌłÆ1 to ŌłÆ2 marks)**.
> 3. **Incorrect number of iterations:** Always compute $n = \frac{X - x_0}{h}$ and verify it is an integer. Off-by-one errors are extremely common. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 4. **Not showing intermediate $f(x_i, y_i)$ values:** KTU examiners award partial credit for displaying slope computations. Always tabulate $x_i, y_i, f(x_i, y_i), h \cdot f(x_i, y_i)$ columns. **(ŌłÆ1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting units in applied problems:** In circuit or thermal problems, students must state units (Volts, Kelvin, etc.) in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist ŌĆö Master These Before Walking into the KTU Exam Hall:**

- **Definition:** Euler's Method is a **first-order**, explicit, one-step ODE solver derived by truncating the Taylor series after the linear term.
- **Master Formula:** $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(x_n, y_n)$ ŌĆö memorize verbatim.
- **Geometric Meaning:** At every step, draw a straight line with slope $f(x_n, y_n)$ through the current point and step forward by $h$.
- **Algorithm Inputs Required:** The function $f(x, y)$, the initial condition $(x_0, y_0)$, the step size $h$, and the target $X$.
- **Local Truncation Error:** $O(h^2)$ ŌĆö comes from the dropped $y''$ term.
- **Global Accumulated Error:** $O(h)$ ŌĆö accumulates over $n$ steps, each contributing $O(h^2)$.
- **Stability:** For $y' = \lambda y$ with $\lambda < 0$, Euler is stable only when $\vert 1 + h\lambda \vert < 1$.
- **Computational Cost:** $n$ slope evaluations per problem. Doubling accuracy doubles the work.
- **Limitation:** Poor accuracy for stiff or long-range problems; superseded by Runge-Kutta 4th order in practice.
- **Advantage:** Conceptually simple, easy to implement, requires minimal memory, ideal for teaching and small embedded systems.
- **Always Tabulate:** Examiners give credit for organized $x_i, y_i, f(x_i, y_i)$ tables.
- **Always Verify:** Cross-check at least the last step against the analytical solution (if available) to compute error.
- **Sign of Error:** Positive error $\Rightarrow$ underestimation; negative error $\Rightarrow$ overestimation. (Convexity of the solution determines which case applies.)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modified Euler's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Modified Euler's Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modified Euler's Method** is a second-order numerical integration technique used to solve an ordinary differential equation (ODE) of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with an initial condition $y(x_0) = y_0$. It belongs to the family of **Runge-Kutta predictor-corrector methods** and provides improved accuracy over the basic Euler's method by averaging the slope of the tangent at the current point with the slope of a predicted (provisional) point. The method employs a **two-stage evaluation** within each subinterval of width $h$: - A **Predictor step** uses the standard Euler formula to obtain a provisional estimate of the next value. - A **Corrector step** refines this estimate by replacing the single slope with the **arithmetic mean** of the slopes at the current and the predicted points. Mathematically, the canonical iteration is written as: $$ \begin{aligned} y_{i+1}^{(0)} &= y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i) \quad \text{(Predictor)} \\ y_{i+1} &= y_i + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f(x_i, y_i) + f\!\left(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}\right) \right] \quad \text{(Corrector)} \end{aligned} $$ where $x_{i+1} = x_i + h$. > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context (KTU Board Reference):** The method is sometimes referred to as **Heun's Method** in literature. It is classified under the broader umbrella of **Runge-Kutta 2nd Order (RK2)** techniques with a predictor-corrector formulation. The local truncation error is of order $O(h^3)$ and the global accumulated error is of order $O(h^2)$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Mountain Climber Imagine you are hiking along an unknown mountain ridge (representing the true solution curve) using a GPS that only tells you your current location and the **gradient (slope) at that point**. - **Basic Euler's Method** is like the naive hiker who, after reading the slope at his current point, strides blindly forward in that single direction for the entire step length $h$. He ends up drifting away from the true path because the slope is changing. - **Modified Euler's Method** is the smarter hiker. He first **takes a tentative stride** in the direction of the current slope (the *predictor*). He then checks the new slope at that predicted point, takes the **average of the initial slope and the predicted slope**, and uses this refined average slope to take his actual step (the *corrector*). This averaging makes his trajectory follow the true ridge much more closely. > [!TIP] > **The Core Idea in One Line:** Instead of using *one* slope for the step, Modified Euler uses the **average of two slopes** ŌĆö one at the start of the interval and one at a predicted end-point. ## 1.3 Why the Method Exists ŌĆö The Accuracy Gap | Method | Local Truncation Error | Global Error | |---|---|---| | Euler's Method | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h)$ | | Modified Euler's Method | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | | Taylor Series (2nd order) | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^2)$ | The Modified Euler's Method matches the **2nd-order Taylor series expansion** without requiring the explicit computation of partial derivatives of $f(x, y)$, making it a much simpler alternative for practical computation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Broken Down Step-by-Step The Modified Euler's Method operates through a strict sequence within each subinterval $[x_i, x_{i+1}]$: 1. **Initial Setup:** - Given: ODE $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$, initial condition $(x_0, y_0)$, step size $h$, and target point $x_n = x_0 + n \cdot h$. 2. **Stage 1 ŌĆö Predictor (First Approximation):** - The standard Euler formula gives a provisional value: - $y_{i+1}^{(0)} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i)$ - This value is *not* the final answer; it is a "guess" used to compute the slope at the end of the interval. 3. **Stage 2 ŌĆö Slope Evaluation at Predicted Point:** - Compute the slope function at the predicted point: - $m_2 = f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})$ - Also retain the slope at the current point: $m_1 = f(x_i, y_i)$. 4. **Stage 3 ŌĆö Corrector (Refined Approximation):** - Average the two slopes and multiply by the step size: - $y_{i+1} = y_i + \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot (m_1 + m_2)$ 5. **Iteration:** - Set $i \leftarrow i+1$ and repeat until the target $x_n$ is reached. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why averaging works:** By the **Mean Value Theorem**, the average slope across an interval closely approximates the true average rate of change $\dfrac{y(x_{i+1}) - y(x_i)}{h}$, provided the function is sufficiently smooth. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Element** | **Mathematical Form** | **Description** | |---|---|---| | ODE Form | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$ | Standard first-order IVP | | Predictor | $y_{i+1}^{(0)} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i)$ | Provisional Euler estimate | | Corrector | $y_{i+1} = y_i + \dfrac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) \right]$ | Refined Modified Euler value | | Slope at $x_i$ | $k_1 = f(x_i, y_i)$ | Initial slope | | Slope at predicted $x_{i+1}$ | $k_2 = f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})$ | Slope at provisional point | | Combined increment | $\Delta y = \dfrac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$ | Trapezoidal-type averaging | | Local Truncation Error | $\tau_i = O(h^3)$ | Error made in *one* step | | Global Error | $E_n = O(h^2)$ | Error accumulated over $n$ steps | | Number of Steps | $n = \dfrac{x_n - x_0}{h}$ | Required subintervals | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in conditions like $x \in [a, b]$ must be written as $\vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Also, use $\dfrac{h}{2}$ instead of inline fractions in the corrector formula for clarity in valuation scripts. ## 2.3 Engineering Applications of the Method The Modified Euler's Method is a workhorse in real-time and computationally efficient simulations: - **Electrical Engineering:** Transient analysis of RC/RL circuits where $V'(t) = -\dfrac{V(t) - V_s}{RC}$. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Vibration analysis of single-degree-of-freedom damped systems $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$ (after reduction to first-order system). - **Chemical Engineering:** Solving rate equations in batch reactors, $\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = -k \cdot C_A^n$. - **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling population dynamics in epidemiology, $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$. - **Control Systems:** Real-time simulation of plant dynamics where higher-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) is computationally expensive. > [!TIP] > The Modified Euler is the **best trade-off** when basic Euler is too inaccurate but RK4 is too computationally heavy (e.g., embedded controllers, real-time hardware-in-loop simulations). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Modified Euler's Corrector Formula We derive the corrector formula rigorously by comparing the iteration to the **2nd-order Taylor series expansion**. ### Step 1: Recall the Taylor Expansion of $y(x_{i+1})$ about $x_i$ $$ y(x_{i+1}) = y(x_i + h) = y(x_i) + h \cdot y'(x_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2!} \cdot y''(x_i) + \dfrac{h^3}{3!} \cdot y'''(x_i) + O(h^4) $$ ### Step 2: Express Derivatives in Terms of $f(x, y)$ Since $y'(x) = f(x, y)$, we differentiate once more to get $y''(x)$ using the **chain rule for total derivatives**: $$ y''(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} f(x, y) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \cdot \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} + f \cdot \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute into the Taylor Series $$ y(x_{i+1}) = y(x_i) + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2}\left( f_x + f \cdot f_y \right)_{(x_i, y_i)} + O(h^3) $$ The term $\left( f_x + f \cdot f_y \right)$ represents the second-order derivative contribution that we wish to *implicitly* capture without explicitly computing the partials. ### Step 4: Two-Variable Taylor Expansion of $f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})$ We expand the function $f$ about $(x_i, y_i)$ along the direction $(h, h \cdot f(x_i, y_i))$, where the direction in $y$ comes from the basic Euler predictor: $$ f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) = f(x_i + h, y_i + h f_i) \approx f_i + h \cdot f_{x_i} + h f_i \cdot f_{y_i} + O(h^2) $$ where $f_i = f(x_i, y_i)$, $f_{x_i} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(x_i, y_i)}$, and $f_{y_i} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}\bigg|_{(x_i, y_i)}$. ### Step 5: Form the Sum of Slopes $$ f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) \approx 2 f_i + h \left( f_{x_i} + f_i \cdot f_{y_i} \right) + O(h^2) $$ ### Step 6: Multiply by $\dfrac{h}{2}$ $$ \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) \right] = h \cdot f_i + \dfrac{h^2}{2}\left( f_{x_i} + f_i \cdot f_{y_i} \right) + O(h^3) $$ ### Step 7: Construct the Corrector $$ y_{i+1} = y_i + h \cdot f_i + \dfrac{h^2}{2}\left( f_{x_i} + f_i \cdot f_{y_i} \right) + O(h^3) $$ ### Step 8: Compare with the Taylor Series from Step 3 We observe that: $$ \boxed{y_{i+1} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2} \cdot y''(x_i) + O(h^3)} $$ This **exactly matches the 2nd-order Taylor series**. Hence, the **local truncation error** (the term we dropped) is of order $O(h^3)$, and the **global error** (accumulated over $n = O(1/h)$ steps) is of order $O(h^2)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Examiner's Note:** The key insight is that the Modified Euler's Method achieves the accuracy of a 2nd-order Taylor series method **without requiring explicit computation of $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ and $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}$**. This is the genius of Runge-Kutta family of methods. --- ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö Complete Solution > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern Style Question]** > Solve $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$, with $y(0) = 1$, using Modified Euler's Method to find $y(0.2)$. Take $h = 0.1$. ### Given Data $$ f(x, y) = x + y, \quad x_0 = 0, \quad y_0 = 1, \quad h = 0.1 $$ ### Iteration 1: From $x_0 = 0$ to $x_1 = 0.1$ **Step A ŌĆö Predictor:** $$ y_1^{(0)} = y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 1 + (0.1)(0 + 1) = 1 + 0.1 = 1.1 $$ **Step B ŌĆö Slope at predicted point:** $$ f(x_1, y_1^{(0)}) = f(0.1, 1.1) = 0.1 + 1.1 = 1.2 $$ **Step C ŌĆö Corrector:** $$ y_1 = y_0 + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f(x_0, y_0) + f(x_1, y_1^{(0)}) \right] $$ $$ y_1 = 1 + \dfrac{0.1}{2}\left[ (0 + 1) + (0.1 + 1.1) \right] = 1 + 0.05 \cdot (1 + 1.2) = 1 + 0.05 \cdot 2.2 = 1 + 0.11 $$ $$ \boxed{y_1 = y(0.1) = 1.11} $$ ### Iteration 2: From $x_1 = 0.1$ to $x_2 = 0.2$ **Step A ŌĆö Predictor:** $$ y_2^{(0)} = y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) = 1.11 + (0.1)(0.1 + 1.11) = 1.11 + 0.1 \cdot 1.21 = 1.11 + 0.121 = 1.231 $$ **Step B ŌĆö Slope at predicted point:** $$ f(x_2, y_2^{(0)}) = f(0.2, 1.231) = 0.2 + 1.231 = 1.431 $$ **Step C ŌĆö Corrector:** $$ y_2 = y_1 + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f(x_1, y_1) + f(x_2, y_2^{(0)}) \right] $$ $$ y_2 = 1.11 + \dfrac{0.1}{2}\left[ (0.1 + 1.11) + (0.2 + 1.231) \right] = 1.11 + 0.05 \cdot (1.21 + 1.431) $$ $$ y_2 = 1.11 + 0.05 \cdot 2.641 = 1.11 + 0.13205 $$ $$ \boxed{y_2 = y(0.2) = 1.24205} $$ ### Verification with Exact Solution The exact solution of $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x + y$ with $y(0) = 1$ is $y(x) = 2e^x - x - 1$. - True value at $x = 0.2$: $y(0.2) = 2e^{0.2} - 0.2 - 1 = 2(1.22140) - 1.2 = 2.44281 - 1.2 = 1.24281$ - Computed value: $1.24205$ - **Absolute Error:** $\vert 1.24281 - 1.24205 \vert = 0.00076$ Ō£ō (excellent accuracy!) --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Step Component** | **Basic Euler** | **Modified Euler** | **2nd-Order Taylor** | |---|---|---|---| | Number of $f$-evaluations per step | **1** | **2** | **2** (plus partials) | | Need for $f_x$ and $f_y$? | No | **No** | **Yes** | | Local error | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^3)$ | | Global error | $O(h)$ | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h^2)$ | | Computational cost per step | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | | Implementation complexity | Trivial | Easy | Moderate (needs partials) | | Best use case | Quick estimates | **Standard balance** | Research/derivation | > [!NOTE] > The Modified Euler's Method **dominates** the 2nd-order Taylor method in practice because it achieves the *same accuracy* with *less analytical effort* (no partial derivatives needed). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Read x0, y0, h, xn] --> B[Compute n = xn - x0 divided by h] B --> C[Initialize i = 0, x = x0, y = y0] C --> D{i less than n?} D -- No --> Z[PRINT final y as y_xn and STOP] D -- Yes --> E["Predictor: yp = y + h * f(x, y)"] E --> F["xp = x + h"] F --> G["Compute f1 = f(x, y) and f2 = f(xp, yp)"] G --> H["Corrector: y_new = y + h/2 * f1 + f2"] H --> I["Update: x = xp, y = y_new"] I --> J[Increment i = i + 1] J --> D styleA[fill:E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px] style E fill:FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px style H fill:E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px style Z fill:FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The **blue-shaded node** (START) marks initialization, the **orange-shaded node** marks the predictor stage, the **green-shaded node** marks the corrector stage, and the **pink-shaded node** marks termination. Students are expected to draw this kind of flowchart for "explain the algorithm" type questions. ## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Diagram ```mermaid graph LR P0[Point x_i, y_i
Slope f_x_i_y_i] -->|Predictor: y plus h*f| P1["Provisional Point
x_i+1, y_i+1 superscript 0
Slope f at provisional"] P1 -->|Average slopes| P2["Refined Point
x_i+1, y_i+1
True solution on curve"] P0 -.->|Trapezoidal averaging| P2 P0 -->|Euler straight line| P1 style P0 fill:BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The provisional point (orange) lies on the line tangent to the curve at $(x_i, y_i)$. The refined point (green) is obtained by *trapezoidal averaging* of the two slopes, which closely follows the true solution curve. ## 4.3 Schematic: Error Order Comparison ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Orders O1[Basic Euler
Local O of h squared
Global O of h] --> O2[Modified Euler
Local O of h cubed
Global O of h squared] O2 --> O3[RK4 Method
Local O of h to the 5
Global O of h to the 4] end style O1 fill:FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px style O2 fill:C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px style O3 fill:BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Each step "up" the Runge-Kutta ladder **doubles the exponent** of the error order. The Modified Euler's Method is the **first major upgrade** over the basic Euler, providing one extra order of accuracy for a modest computational increase (only one extra function evaluation per step). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** > **"Write the predictor and corrector formulas of the Modified Euler's Method. Mention the order of its local and global truncation errors."** **Model Answer:** The Modified Euler's Method for solving $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$ and step size $h$ is given by: **Predictor formula:** $$ y_{i+1}^{(0)} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i) $$ **Corrector formula:** $$ y_{i+1} = y_i + \dfrac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) \right] $$ **Order of errors:** - Local Truncation Error: $O(h^3)$ - Global Accumulated Error: $O(h^2)$ The method belongs to the **2nd-order Runge-Kutta (RK2)** family and is also known as **Heun's Method**. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** > **"How does the Modified Euler's Method differ from the basic Euler's Method in terms of slope evaluation? Why is it more accurate?"** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Basic Euler's Method** | **Modified Euler's Method** | |---|---|---| | Number of slopes per step | **One** slope at $(x_i, y_i)$ | **Two** slopes ŌĆö at $(x_i, y_i)$ and the predicted point $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})$ | | Approximation strategy | Uses $f(x_i, y_i)$ alone | Uses $\dfrac{f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})}{2}$ (average) | | Global error order | $O(h)$ | $O(h^2)$ | **Reason for higher accuracy:** The Modified Euler's Method incorporates the **arithmetic mean** of slopes at the start and the (predictor-estimated) end of the interval. This effectively captures the second-order derivative term $\dfrac{h^2}{2} y''(x_i)$ that the basic Euler method ignores. As a result, the local truncation error improves from $O(h^2)$ to $O(h^3)$, and global error improves from $O(h)$ to $O(h^2)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - End Semester - Module 3 Pattern]** > *Internal Choice: Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.* --- ### ŌØæ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Computational Application > **[KTU ESE - Model Question 1]** > **(a)** Using the Modified Euler's Method, find an approximate value of $y$ at $x = 0.4$ for the differential equation $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = x^2 + y^2$ with $y(0) = 0$. Take step size $h = 0.2$. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** Compare your result with the value obtained using the basic Euler's Method. What inference do you draw regarding accuracy? *(7 marks)* #### Part (a) ŌĆö Modified Euler's Method Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** $$ f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2, \quad x_0 = 0, \quad y_0 = 0, \quad h = 0.2, \quad x_{\text{target}} = 0.4 $$ **Number of steps:** $n = \dfrac{0.4 - 0}{0.2} = 2$ --- **Iteration 1: $x_0 = 0 \longrightarrow x_1 = 0.2$** Predictor: $$ y_1^{(0)} = y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 0 + (0.2)(0^2 + 0^2) = 0 $$ Slope at predicted point: $$ f(x_1, y_1^{(0)}) = f(0.2, 0) = (0.2)^2 + 0^2 = 0.04 $$ Corrector: $$ y_1 = y_0 + \dfrac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_0, y_0) + f(x_1, y_1^{(0)}) \right] = 0 + 0.1 \cdot (0 + 0.04) = 0.004 $$ $$ \boxed{y_1 = y(0.2) = 0.004} $$ --- **Iteration 2: $x_1 = 0.2 \longrightarrow x_2 = 0.4$** Predictor: $$ y_2^{(0)} = y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) = 0.004 + (0.2)\left[ (0.2)^2 + (0.004)^2 \right] $$ $$ y_2^{(0)} = 0.004 + 0.2 \cdot (0.04 + 0.000016) = 0.004 + 0.2 \cdot 0.040016 = 0.004 + 0.0080032 = 0.0120032 $$ Slope at predicted point: $$ f(x_2, y_2^{(0)}) = f(0.4, 0.0120032) = (0.4)^2 + (0.0120032)^2 = 0.16 + 0.00014408 = 0.16014408 $$ Corrector: $$ y_2 = y_1 + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f(x_1, y_1) + f(x_2, y_2^{(0)}) \right] $$ $$ y_2 = 0.004 + 0.1 \cdot (0.040016 + 0.16014408) = 0.004 + 0.1 \cdot 0.20016008 $$ $$ y_2 = 0.004 + 0.020016008 = 0.024016008 $$ $$ \boxed{y_2 = y(0.4) \approx 0.02402 \text{ (Modified Euler)}} $$ --- #### Part (b) ŌĆö Comparison with Basic Euler's Method (7 Marks) **Basic Euler Iterations:** Iteration 1: $y_1 = y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 0 + 0.2 \cdot 0 = 0$ Iteration 2: $y_2 = y_1 + h \cdot f(x_1, y_1) = 0 + 0.2 \cdot (0.04 + 0) = 0.008$ $$ \boxed{y_2^{\text{Euler}} = y(0.4) \approx 0.008 \text{ (Basic Euler)}} $$ **Comparison Table:** | **Method** | **Approximate $y(0.4)$** | |---|---| | Basic Euler's Method | $0.00800$ | | Modified Euler's Method | $0.02402$ | | Difference | $0.01602$ | **Inference:** The Modified Euler's Method yields a value **three times larger** than the basic Euler's Method. Since the function $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$ is rapidly increasing (quadratic growth), the basic Euler method ŌĆö which uses only the *initial* slope ŌĆö drastically underestimates the true growth. The Modified Euler's Method, by incorporating the slope at the predicted endpoint, captures this **convex (accelerating) behavior** much more accurately. This demonstrates the **second-order accuracy** of the Modified Euler's Method over the first-order basic Euler. --- ### ŌØæ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice (Theory + Derivation) > **[KTU ESE - Model Question 2]** > **(a)** Derive the Modified Euler's corrector formula by comparing the iteration to the second-order Taylor series expansion. Clearly show that the local truncation error is $O(h^3)$. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** For the IVP $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 1 - y$, $y(0) = 2$, find $y(0.4)$ using $h = 0.2$ via the Modified Euler's Method. *(7 marks)* #### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Taylor series of true solution: $$ y(x_{i+1}) = y(x_i) + h y'(x_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2} y''(x_i) + O(h^3) $$ **Step 2:** Express derivatives in terms of $f(x, y)$: $$ y'(x_i) = f(x_i, y_i), \quad y''(x_i) = f_x + f \cdot f_y $$ **Step 3:** Substituting, $$ y(x_{i+1}) = y(x_i) + h f(x_i, y_i) + \dfrac{h^2}{2}\left[ f_x + f f_y \right]_{(x_i, y_i)} + O(h^3) $$ **Step 4:** Apply two-variable Taylor expansion to $f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)})$ where $y_{i+1}^{(0)} = y_i + h f(x_i, y_i)$: $$ f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) = f_i + h f_{x_i} + h f_i f_{y_i} + O(h^2) $$ **Step 5:** Sum and average: $$ \dfrac{1}{2}\left[ f_i + f_{i+1} \right] = f_i + \dfrac{h}{2}\left[ f_{x_i} + f_i f_{y_i} \right] + O(h^2) $$ **Step 6:** Multiply by $h$ and add $y_i$: $$ y_{i+1} = y_i + h f_i + \dfrac{h^2}{2}\left[ f_{x_i} + f_i f_{y_i} \right] + O(h^3) $$ **Step 7:** Comparing with Step 3, the iteration **matches the Taylor series up to the $h^2$ term**. The leading error term is $O(h^3)$, confirming that the **local truncation error is $O(h^3)$** and hence the method is a 2nd-order method. $\blacksquare$ --- #### Part (b) ŌĆö Computational Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** $f(x, y) = 1 - y$, $x_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 2$, $h = 0.2$, target: $x = 0.4$. **Iteration 1: $x_0 = 0 \longrightarrow x_1 = 0.2$** Predictor: $$ y_1^{(0)} = y_0 + h \cdot f(x_0, y_0) = 2 + 0.2 \cdot (1 - 2) = 2 + 0.2(-1) = 2 - 0.2 = 1.8 $$ Slope at predicted point: $$ f(x_1, y_1^{(0)}) = 1 - 1.8 = -0.8 $$ Corrector: $$ y_1 = 2 + \dfrac{0.2}{2} \left[ (1 - 2) + (1 - 1.8) \right] = 2 + 0.1 \cdot (-1 + (-0.8)) = 2 + 0.1 \cdot (-1.8) $$ $$ y_1 = 2 - 0.18 = 1.82 $$ $$ \boxed{y_1 = y(0.2) = 1.82} $$ **Iteration 2: $x_1 = 0.2 \longrightarrow x_2 = 0.4$** Predictor: $$ y_2^{(0)} = 1.82 + 0.2 \cdot (1 - 1.82) = 1.82 + 0.2 \cdot (-0.82) = 1.82 - 0.164 = 1.656 $$ Slope at predicted point: $$ f(x_2, y_2^{(0)}) = 1 - 1.656 = -0.656 $$ Corrector: $$ y_2 = 1.82 + 0.1 \cdot \left[ (1 - 1.82) + (1 - 1.656) \right] = 1.82 + 0.1 \cdot (-0.82 + (-0.656)) $$ $$ y_2 = 1.82 + 0.1 \cdot (-1.476) = 1.82 - 0.1476 = 1.6724 $$ $$ \boxed{y_2 = y(0.4) \approx 1.6724 \text{ (Modified Euler)}} $$ **Verification (Exact solution):** $y(x) = 1 + e^{-x}$, so $y(0.4) = 1 + e^{-0.4} = 1 + 0.67032 = 1.67032$. - **Error of Modified Euler:** $\vert 1.67032 - 1.6724 \vert = 0.00208$ Ō£ō (very small) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing the predictor with the final answer:** Many students write $y_{i+1}^{(0)}$ as the final $y_{i+1}$, losing 3ŌĆō4 marks. The predictor is only a *provisional* value used to compute the slope at the end of the interval. > 2. **Forgetting to use the predicted $y$ when computing the second slope:** A frequent error is using $y_i$ (instead of $y_{i+1}^{(0)}$) in $f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$. This reduces the method to the basic Euler and **fetches 0 marks** for the corrector step. > 3. **Off-by-one in step counter:** Ensure the predictor's $x_{i+1}$ is correctly computed as $x_i + h$, *not* $x_{i+1}$ from a previous iteration. > 4. **Arithmetic slips in slope evaluation:** When $f(x, y)$ involves products (e.g., $x^2$, $y^2$, $xy$), students often miss parentheses. Show full substitution explicitly. > 5. **Not reporting both errors in the formula sheet questions:** Always state BOTH local $O(h^3)$ and global $O(h^2)$ ŌĆö partial credit is given only if both are mentioned. > 6. **Skipping the predictor step in the derivation:** A full derivation question (Part A of Q.B) requires showing the predictor-corrector relationship, not just the final corrector formula. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Modified Euler's Method** - Ō£ģ **Definition:** Second-order Runge-Kutta method using a predictor-corrector framework to solve first-order IVPs. - Ō£ģ **Predictor:** $y_{i+1}^{(0)} = y_i + h \cdot f(x_i, y_i)$ ŌĆö uses *only* the slope at the current point. - Ō£ģ **Corrector:** $y_{i+1} = y_i + \dfrac{h}{2} \left[ f(x_i, y_i) + f(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1}^{(0)}) \right]$ ŌĆö uses the *average* of two slopes. - Ō£ģ **Slope evaluations per step:** **Two** (one at start, one at predicted end). - Ō£ģ **Local Truncation Error:** $O(h^3)$ ŌĆö matches 2nd-order Taylor series. - Ō£ģ **Global Error:** $O(h^2)$ ŌĆö second-order method. - Ō£ģ **No partial derivatives required** ŌĆö unlike the 2nd-order Taylor series method. - Ō£ģ **Procedure per step:** Predict $\rightarrow$ Evaluate slope at predicted point $\rightarrow$ Correct by averaging. - Ō£ģ **Step count formula:** $n = \dfrac{x_n - x_0}{h}$. - Ō£ģ **Naming:** Also called **Heun's Method** or **RK2 (predictor-corrector form)**. - Ō£ģ **Improvement over basic Euler:** Captures the $h^2$ term in Taylor series, providing a global error that is *one order higher* in $h$. - Ō£ģ **Computational cost:** Approximately **2├Ś the basic Euler**, but accuracy is dramatically better for the same $h$. - Ō£ģ **When to use:** Ideal for problems with **moderate accuracy requirements** where RK4 is overkill, e.g., embedded systems, real-time simulations, simple circuit analysis. - Ō£ģ **When NOT to use:** For highly stiff ODEs (use implicit methods), or when extreme accuracy is needed (use RK4 or higher). - Ō£ģ **Board Tip:** Always write the predictor formula *before* the corrector formula in your answer sheet. The KTU examiner scans for this sequence in the first 30 seconds of valuation.

topics.title: Fourth Order Runge-Kutta Method, Higher Order Equations of Initial Value Type by Runge-Kutta Method, Ordinary Differential Equations of the Boundary Value Type - Finite Difference Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fourth Order Runge-Kutta Method, Higher Order Equations of Initial Value Type by Runge-Kutta Method, Ordinary Differential Equations of the Boundary Value Type - Finite Difference Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta (RK4) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The Fourth-Order Runge-Kutta method is a single-step, explicit, iterative numerical procedure used to obtain the solution of an **Initial Value Problem (IVP)** of the form $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x, y)$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$, by combining four function evaluations (called *stages* or *k-values*) at carefully chosen sub-points within each interval, achieving a local truncation error of order $O(h^5)$ and a global error of order $O(h^4)$.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Four Witnesses of a Journey"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a hiker walking from point $x_n$ to $x_{n+1}$ along an unknown hill whose slope at any point is $f(x, y)$. The hiker cannot see the whole hill. To estimate the new position, he asks **four witnesses** about the slope at four different strategic points:
> - Witness 1 ($k_1$): The slope at the *starting point* itself.
> - Witness 2 ($k_2$): The slope at the *midpoint*, using witness 1 to guess position.
> - Witness 3 ($k_3$): The slope at the *midpoint again*, but using witness 2 to guess position.
> - Witness 4 ($k_4$): The slope at the *end point*, using witness 3 to guess position.
>
> He then takes a *weighted average* of these four slope reports (weights $1, 2, 2, 1$) ŌĆö giving the middle witnesses double importance ŌĆö and uses that average slope to leap from $x_n$ to $x_{n+1}$. The result is astonishingly accurate even for large step sizes.

### Why "Fourth-Order"?

The name **"Fourth-Order"** has two meanings a KTU examiner will test:
1. **Algebraic Order:** The global error behaves like $C h^4$, so halving the step size reduces error by a factor of **16**.
2. **Number of Stages:** It requires exactly **4 functional evaluations** per step ($k_1, k_2, k_3, k_4$).

---

## 1.2 Higher-Order Equations of the Initial Value Type ŌĆö Solved by RK4

A *higher-order* IVP is an ODE of order $n \geq 2$ together with its initial conditions. Examples:

$$y'' = f(x, y, y'), \quad y(x_0) = y_0, \quad y'(x_0) = y_0'$$

$$y''' = f(x, y, y', y''), \quad y(x_0) = y_0, \quad y'(x_0) = y_0', \quad y''(x_0) = y_0''$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Strategy:** A higher-order ODE is NEVER solved directly. It is first **reduced to an equivalent system of first-order ODEs** by introducing new dependent variables for each derivative, and then RK4 is applied to the entire system simultaneously.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Translating a Multi-Layer Cake into a Stack of Pancakes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A 3rd-order ODE is like a 3-layer cake ŌĆö the layers are coupled and hard to bite into directly. By *defining* the first derivative as a new variable, the second derivative as another new variable, etc., we *slice* the cake horizontally into 3 thin pancakes (first-order equations). Now we apply the same RK4 method to *each pancake* at every step, but **advancing them together** in lockstep. The "linking" of pancakes corresponds to how $y_1' = y_2$ and $y_2' = y_3$, etc.

---

## 1.3 Ordinary Differential Equations of the Boundary Value Type (BVP) ŌĆö Finite Difference Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A *Boundary Value Problem* is an ODE in which the **auxiliary conditions (boundary conditions) are specified at two or more distinct values of the independent variable**, typically the endpoints of the interval. The Finite Difference Method (FDM) replaces the derivatives in the ODE with their **finite difference approximations** built from discrete node values, transforming the continuous differential equation into a system of simultaneous algebraic (linear or non-linear) equations that can be solved by standard matrix techniques.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "A Stretched Elastic String Pinned at Both Ends"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a guitar string stretched between two fixed pegs. We do not know its shape in between. We *measure* the height of the string at $N+1$ equally spaced pegs. Using the *curvature* of the string at the middle peg ($y'' \approx$ second difference) and the *known heights* at both end pegs (the boundary conditions), we can write one algebraic equation per middle peg. With $N-1$ unknowns and $N-1$ equations, we solve the whole shape at once. This is the FDM spirit.

### Classification of Boundary Conditions (Critical for KTU)

- **Dirichlet BC:** $y(a) = \alpha$ and $y(b) = \beta$ ŌĆö the *values* are prescribed.
- **Neumann BC:** $y'(a) = \alpha$ and $y'(b) = \beta$ ŌĆö the *slopes* are prescribed.
- **Mixed / Robin BC:** $a_1 y(a) + a_2 y'(a) = c_1$ ŌĆö a *linear combination* is prescribed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of the RK4 Method

### Why are the $k_i$ "staged" this way?

The RK4 method is derived by matching the Taylor series expansion of the exact solution $y(x_{n+1})$ about $x_n$ through the $h^4$ term, using a weighted combination of slopes sampled at strategic sub-interval points. The optimal staging was discovered by **Carl Runge** (1895) and **Martin Wilhelm Kutta** (1901).

**The four stages, with their precise meaning:**

| Stage | Geometric Role | Math Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $k_1$ | Slope at the *start* of the interval | $k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$ |
| $k_2$ | Slope at the *midpoint*, using $k_1$ as predictor | $k_2 = h f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$ |
| $k_3$ | Slope at the *midpoint*, using $k_2$ as predictor | $k_3 = h f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)$ |
| $k_4$ | Slope at the *end* of the interval, using $k_3$ as predictor | $k_4 = h f\!\left(x_n + h,\, y_n + k_3\right)$ |

**Final update rule:**

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{6}\!\left(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4\right)$$

### Why weights are $1, 2, 2, 1$ (Simpson's 1/3 rule in disguise!)

> [!NOTE]
> The weighting $\frac{1}{6}(1 + 2 + 2 + 1) = 1$ is a numerical quadrature (Simpson's rule) applied *to the slope function* over $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$. The midpoint values receive more weight because they are *more representative* of the average slope over the interval.

### Error Characteristics

| Property | RK2 | RK3 | **RK4** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stages per step | 2 | 3 | **4** |
| Local truncation error | $O(h^3)$ | $O(h^4)$ | $\mathbf{O(h^5)}$ |
| Global truncation error | $O(h^2)$ | $O(h^3)$ | $\mathbf{O(h^4)}$ |
| Function evaluations per step | 2 | 3 | **4** |

---

## 2.2 Reduction of Higher-Order IVPs to First-Order Systems

### Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define new variables.** For an $n$-th order ODE, define $n$ new variables equal to successive derivatives of $y$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Form the system.** Each new variable's derivative is, by definition, the next one in the chain, except the last which equals the original RHS.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Initial conditions.** Translate the original initial conditions into initial values of the new variables.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply RK4 vector-wise.** Run RK4 once for every component of the system, but at every step using the *current* values of all components.

### Worked Template ŌĆö Second-Order IVP

Given: $y'' = f(x, y, y')$ with $y(x_0) = y_0$ and $y'(x_0) = y_0'$.

- Set $y_1 = y$ and $y_2 = y'$.
- Then the system is:

$$y_1' = y_2, \qquad y_2' = f(x, y_1, y_2)$$

- Initial vector: $\mathbf{y}(x_0) = \begin{pmatrix} y_0 \\ y_0' \end{pmatrix}$.

- RK4 for the system at step $n$:

$$\mathbf{k}_1 = h\, \mathbf{F}(x_n, \mathbf{y}_n)$$

$$\mathbf{k}_2 = h\, \mathbf{F}\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, \mathbf{y}_n + \tfrac{\mathbf{k}_1}{2}\right)$$

$$\mathbf{k}_3 = h\, \mathbf{F}\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\, \mathbf{y}_n + \tfrac{\mathbf{k}_2}{2}\right)$$

$$\mathbf{k}_4 = h\, \mathbf{F}\!\left(x_n + h,\, \mathbf{y}_n + \mathbf{k}_3\right)$$

$$\mathbf{y}_{n+1} = \mathbf{y}_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(\mathbf{k}_1 + 2\mathbf{k}_2 + 2\mathbf{k}_3 + \mathbf{k}_4)$$

where the vector field $\mathbf{F}(x, \mathbf{y}) = \begin{pmatrix} y_2 \\ f(x, y_1, y_2) \end{pmatrix}$.

---

## 2.3 Finite Difference Method for Second-Order BVPs

### Core Finite Difference Approximations (KTU Mandatory)

For a sufficiently smooth function $y(x)$ on a uniform grid with spacing $h$:

$$y'(x_i) \;\approx\; \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2h} \quad \text{(Central Difference ŌĆö second order accurate)}$$

$$y''(x_i) \;\approx\; \frac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} \quad \text{(Central Difference ŌĆö second order accurate)}$$

### Canonical BVP Form

$$y'' = f(x, y), \quad y(a) = \alpha, \quad y(b) = \beta$$

Substituting the central-difference approximations:

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} = f(x_i, y_i)$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1} = h^2 f(x_i, y_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

This produces $N-1$ linear (or non-linear) equations in the $N-1$ unknowns $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{N-1}$.

### General Linear BVP

$$y'' + P(x)\, y' + Q(x)\, y = R(x)$$

After substitution and multiplying through by $h^2$:

$$\left(1 - \tfrac{h}{2} P_i\right) y_{i-1} + \left(-2 + h^2 Q_i\right) y_i + \left(1 + \tfrac{h}{2} P_i\right) y_{i+1} = h^2 R_i$$

### Matrix Form

For an $(N+1)$-point grid with interior unknowns $y_1, \ldots, y_{N-1}$:

$$\mathbf{A}\, \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{B}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is a **tridiagonal matrix** of size $(N-1) \times (N-1)$, $\mathbf{Y} = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{N-1})^T$, and $\mathbf{B}$ incorporates both the right-hand-side $h^2 R_i$ and the boundary values at $i = 0$ and $i = N$.

### Neumann Boundary Condition (one-sided differences)

If $y'(a) = \alpha$, the **forward difference** at $i = 0$ gives:

$$\frac{-3y_0 + 4y_1 - y_2}{2h} = \alpha \quad \text{(2nd order forward formula)}$$

Similarly for $y'(b) = \beta$ at $i = N$:

$$\frac{3y_N - 4y_{N-1} + y_{N-2}}{2h} = \beta$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Day Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> Save these for last-minute revision. The pipe `|` has been replaced by `\vert` to keep the table intact.

| Topic | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| RK4 Stage 1 | $k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$ | Slope at start |
| RK4 Stage 2 | $k_2 = h f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + k_1/2)$ | Midpoint using $k_1$ |
| RK4 Stage 3 | $k_3 = h f(x_n + h/2,\; y_n + k_2/2)$ | Midpoint using $k_2$ |
| RK4 Stage 4 | $k_4 = h f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3)$ | Slope at end using $k_3$ |
| RK4 Update | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \tfrac{1}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)$ | Weighted avg |
| RK4 Local Error | $O(h^5)$ | Per step |
| RK4 Global Error | $O(h^4)$ | Accumulated |
| 2nd Order $\rightarrow$ 1st Order | $y_1 = y,\; y_2 = y'$; system: $y_1' = y_2,\; y_2' = f(x,y_1,y_2)$ | Standard reduction |
| 3rd Order $\rightarrow$ 1st Order | $y_1 = y,\; y_2 = y',\; y_3 = y''$; system: $y_1' = y_2,\; y_2' = y_3,\; y_3' = f(x,y_1,y_2,y_3)$ | Extend further |
| 1st Derivative (CD) | $y'(x_i) \approx (y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}) / (2h)$ | Central Diff |
| 2nd Derivative (CD) | $y''(x_i) \approx (y_{i+1} - 2y_i + y_{i-1}) / h^2$ | Central Diff |
| Forward Diff (1st) | $y'(x_i) \approx (-3y_i + 4y_{i+1} - y_{i+2}) / (2h)$ | Neumann at left end |
| Backward Diff (1st) | $y'(x_i) \approx (3y_i - 4y_{i-1} + y_{i-2}) / (2h)$ | Neumann at right end |
| Linear BVP FDM | $(1 - hP_i/2)\, y_{i-1} + (-2 + h^2 Q_i)\, y_i + (1 + hP_i/2)\, y_{i+1} = h^2 R_i$ | Tridiagonal system |
| Dirichlet BC | $y_0 = \alpha,\; y_N = \beta$ | Used in RHS vector |
| Neumann BC | $y'(a) = \alpha$ or $y'(b) = \beta$ | One-sided diff |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **RK4:** Used in flight-simulator physics, orbital mechanics (spacecraft trajectory propagation), real-time control systems, robotics path planning, and solving non-linear vibration ODEs in mechanical engineering.
> - **Higher-Order Systems:** Every multi-body dynamics problem (vehicle suspension, robotic arm control) is a higher-order IVP.
> - **BVP-FDM:** Steady-state heat conduction in a fin, deflection of a beam with fixed/fixed ends, Laplace/Poisson equations in 1-D, and pressure distribution in porous media flow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RK4 Coefficients via Taylor Series Matching

We want a single-step update $y_{n+1} = y_n + a\, k_1 + b\, k_2 + c\, k_3 + d\, k_4$ with four stages, such that when expanded in Taylor series, the difference from the true solution $y(x_{n+1})$ has no terms lower than $h^5$.

### Setting Up the Stages

Let

$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h f(x_n + \alpha h,\; y_n + \beta k_1)$$

$$k_3 = h f(x_n + \alpha' h,\; y_n + \beta' k_1 + \beta'' k_2)$$

$$k_4 = h f(x_n + \alpha'' h,\; y_n + \beta_1 k_1 + \beta_2 k_2 + \beta_3 k_3)$$

with the update

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + a_1 k_1 + a_2 k_2 + a_3 k_3 + a_4 k_4$$

### Expanding Each Stage About $(x_n, y_n)$

Using $f(x_n + \Delta x,\; y_n + \Delta y) = f + f_x \Delta x + f_y \Delta y + \tfrac{1}{2}(f_{xx} \Delta x^2 + 2 f_{xy} \Delta x \Delta y + f_{yy} \Delta y^2) + \ldots$, with $f = f(x_n, y_n)$:

**Stage $k_1$:**

$$k_1 = h f$$

**Stage $k_2$** with $\Delta x = \alpha h$, $\Delta y = \beta k_1 = \beta h f$:

$$k_2 = h\!\left[ f + f_x (\alpha h) + f_y (\beta h f) + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{xx} (\alpha h)^2 + f_{xy} (\alpha h)(\beta h f) + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{yy} (\beta h f)^2 + \ldots \right]$$

$$k_2 = h f + \alpha h^2 f_x + \beta h^2 f f_y + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 h^3 f_{xx} + \alpha\beta\, h^3 f f_{xy} + \tfrac{1}{2}\beta^2 h^3 f^2 f_{yy} + \ldots$$

**Stage $k_3$** with $\Delta x = \alpha' h$, $\Delta y = \beta' k_1 + \beta'' k_2$. Choosing $\alpha' = \alpha$, $\beta' = 0$, $\beta'' = \beta$ (standard RK4 ansatz) and noting $k_2 = h f + O(h^2)$:

$$\Delta y = \beta'' k_2 = \beta h f + O(h^2)$$

$$k_3 = h f + \alpha h^2 f_x + \beta h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

**Stage $k_4$** with $\alpha'' = 1$, $\beta_1 = 0$, $\beta_2 = 0$, $\beta_3 = 1$:

$$k_4 = h f(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3) = h f + h^2 f_x + h^2 f_y k_3/h + O(h^3)$$

Using $k_3/h = f + O(h)$:

$$k_4 = h f + h^2 f_x + h^2 f f_y + O(h^3)$$

### Combining the Stages

The full increment is

$$\Delta y = a_1 k_1 + a_2 k_2 + a_3 k_3 + a_4 k_4$$

**Coefficient of $h f$:** $\;a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + a_4 = 1$

**Coefficient of $h^2 f_x$:** $\;\alpha a_2 + \alpha a_3 + a_4 = \tfrac{1}{2}$

**Coefficient of $h^2 f f_y$:** $\;\beta a_2 + \beta a_3 + a_4 = \tfrac{1}{2}$

**Coefficient of $h^3$ terms (multiple, e.g., $f_{xx}$):** $\;\tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 a_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 a_3 + \tfrac{1}{2} a_4 = \tfrac{1}{6}$

A standard set of solutions is

$$a_1 = a_4 = \tfrac{1}{6}, \quad a_2 = a_3 = \tfrac{2}{6} = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad \alpha = \alpha' = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \beta = \beta'' = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

with the remaining $\beta' = 0$, $\beta_1 = 0$, $\beta_2 = 0$, $\beta_3 = 1$.

### Final Closed Form

$$\boxed{\; y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{1}{6}\!\left(k_1 + 2 k_2 + 2 k_3 + k_4\right) \;}$$

where

$$k_1 = h f(x_n, y_n)$$

$$k_2 = h f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_1}{2}\right)$$

$$k_3 = h f\!\left(x_n + \tfrac{h}{2},\; y_n + \tfrac{k_2}{2}\right)$$

$$k_4 = h f\!\left(x_n + h,\; y_n + k_3\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation shows that the RK4 method **truncates the Taylor expansion at $h^4$**, so the local error per step is $O(h^5)$. Because there are $O(1/h)$ steps, the global error over a fixed interval is $O(h^4)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tridiagonal System from a Linear Second-Order BVP

**Given BVP:**

$$y'' + P(x)\, y' + Q(x)\, y = R(x), \quad y(a) = \alpha, \quad y(b) = \beta$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discretize the interval.** Let $h = (b - a) / N$ and $x_i = a + i h$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, N$, with $y_i \approx y(x_i)$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply central difference to $y''$:**

$$y''(x_i) \;\approx\; \frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply central difference to $y'$:**

$$y'(x_i) \;\approx\; \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2 h}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$\frac{y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1}}{h^2} + P_i \cdot \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{2 h} + Q_i\, y_i = R_i$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply through by $h^2$:**

$$y_{i+1} - 2 y_i + y_{i-1} + \frac{h P_i}{2}\,(y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}) + h^2 Q_i\, y_i = h^2 R_i$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Collect coefficients of $y_{i-1}, y_i, y_{i+1}$:**

$$\left(1 - \frac{h P_i}{2}\right) y_{i-1} + \left(-2 + h^2 Q_i\right) y_i + \left(1 + \frac{h P_i}{2}\right) y_{i+1} = h^2 R_i$$

This is the **fundamental recurrence** for any linear second-order BVP. The same derivation generalizes to non-linear BVPs $y'' = f(x, y, y')$ by directly substituting the central differences and yielding a (generally) non-linear algebraic equation per interior node, solvable by Newton's method or simple fixed-point iteration.

### Tridiagonal Matrix Form

For $N - 1$ interior unknowns:

$$\begin{pmatrix} d_1 & c_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ a_2 & d_2 & c_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & a_3 & d_3 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & c_{N-2} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & a_{N-1} & d_{N-1} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ y_3 \\ \vdots \\ y_{N-1} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \\ \vdots \\ b_{N-1} \end{pmatrix}$$

where for each $i = 1, \ldots, N-1$:

$$a_i = 1 - \frac{h P_i}{2}, \qquad d_i = -2 + h^2 Q_i, \qquad c_i = 1 + \frac{h P_i}{2}, \qquad b_i = h^2 R_i$$

with adjustments to $b_1$ and $b_{N-1}$ to absorb the known boundary values:

$$b_1 \leftarrow b_1 - a_1\, y_0 = b_1 - a_1\, \alpha$$

$$b_{N-1} \leftarrow b_{N-1} - c_{N-1}\, y_N = b_{N-1} - c_{N-1}\, \beta$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because the matrix is *tridiagonal*, it is solved in $O(N)$ operations using the **Thomas Algorithm** (a streamlined form of Gaussian elimination) ŌĆö not the full $O(N^3)$ matrix inversion.

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RK4 Algorithm ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Inputs x0, y0, h, xN, f]) --> B[Compute number of steps N = ceil xN - x0 divided by h]
    B --> C[Initialize n = 0, xn = x0, yn = y0]
    C --> D[Stage 1: k1 = h times f of xn, yn]
    D --> E[Stage 2: k2 = h times f of xn + h over 2, yn + k1 over 2]
    E --> F[Stage 3: k3 = h times f of xn + h over 2, yn + k2 over 2]
    F --> G[Stage 4: k4 = h times f of xn + h, yn + k3]
    G --> H[Combine: yn+1 = yn + 1 by 6 times k1 + 2k2 + 2k3 + k4]
    H --> I[Advance: xn = xn + h, n = n + 1]
    I --> J{Condition: n less than N?}
    J -- Yes --> D
    J -- No --> K([Output: vector of xn, yn pairs])
    K --> L([End])
```

## 4.2 Higher-Order ODE Reduction ŌĆö Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Original higher-order IVP]) --> B[Identify order n and initial conditions]
    B --> C[Define n auxiliary variables: y1 = y, y2 = y-prime, y3 = y-double-prime, ...]
    C --> D[Build first-order system: y1-prime = y2, y2-prime = y3, ..., yn-prime = f of x, y1, y2, ..., yn]
    D --> E[Build initial vector y-vector at x0 from given conditions]
    E --> F[Apply RK4 as a coupled vector iteration]
    F --> G[Recover y and its derivatives from components of y-vector]
    G --> H([Solution: y, y-prime, y-double-prime at all points])
```

## 4.3 BVP Finite Difference Solution ŌĆö Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Inputs: ODE, interval a, b, boundary conditions, N]) --> B[Step size: h = b - a divided by N]
    B --> C[Create grid x_i for i = 0, 1, ..., N]
    C --> D[Set y_0 = alpha, y_N = beta from Dirichlet BC]
    D --> E[For each interior node i, write central-difference equation]
    E --> F[Build tridiagonal matrix A and RHS vector b]
    F --> G{Is system non-linear?}
    G -- No --> H[Solve A times y-vector = b using Thomas Algorithm]
    G -- Yes --> I[Initialize guess; iterate with Newton-Raphson or fixed-point]
    I --> H
    H --> J([Output: y-vector at all interior nodes])
```

## 4.4 Boundary Condition Handling ŌĆö Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Given BVP]) --> B{Type of boundary condition?}
    B -- Dirichlet: y specified --> C[y_0 = alpha, y_N = beta; move to RHS]
    B -- Neumann: y-prime specified --> D[Use one-sided difference at endpoint]
    B -- Robin: a1 y + a2 y-prime = c --> E[Combine the two formulas at the endpoint]
    C --> F[Build tridiagonal system]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Solve and recover solution]
```
