# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Capacity and Critical Density (Greenshields)

We want to maximize the flux $q(\rho)$.

**Step 1:** Start from the Greenshields flux.

$$q(\rho) = v_f \rho \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_j}\right) = v_f \rho - \dfrac{v_f}{\rho_j} \rho^2$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $\rho$ and set equal to zero.

$$\dfrac{dq}{d\rho} = v_f - \dfrac{2 v_f}{\rho_j} \rho = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the critical density.

$$v_f = \dfrac{2 v_f}{\rho_j} \rho_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_c = \dfrac{\rho_j}{2}$$

**Step 4:** The critical speed is the speed evaluated at $\rho_c$.

$$v_c = v_f \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_j}\right) = v_f \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{v_f}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum flow (capacity) is $q_c = \rho_c \cdot v_c$.

$$q_{max} = \dfrac{\rho_j}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_f}{2} = \dfrac{v_f \rho_j}{4}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Shock Wave Speed (RankineŌĆōHugoniot Condition)

**Step 1:** Integrate the conservation law across a discontinuity at $x = s(t)$.

$$\int_{x_L}^{x_R} \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dx + \int_{x_L}^{x_R} \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x}\, dx = 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Leibniz rule and the fundamental theorem of calculus.

$$-\dot{s}(\rho_R - \rho_L) + (q_R - q_L) = 0$$

where $\dot{s} = ds/dt$ is the shock speed.

**Step 3:** Solve for $\dot{s}$.

$$\dot{s} = \dfrac{q_R - q_L}{\rho_R - \rho_L}$$

**Step 4:** Geometric interpretation ŌĆö the shock speed equals the slope of the chord connecting points $(\rho_L, q_L)$ and $(\rho_R, q_R)$ on the fundamental diagram.

$$\dot{s} = \dfrac{q_R - q_L}{\rho_R - \rho_L} = \text{slope of secant line on the } q\text{ŌĆō}\rho \text{ curve}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Finite Difference Discretization of LWR

**Step 1:** Define the computational grid with uniform spacing $\Delta x$ in space and $\Delta t$ in time.

$$x_i = i \Delta x, \quad t^n = n \Delta t, \quad \rho_i^n \approx \rho(x_i, t^n)$$

**Step 2:** Discretize the LWR equation in conservative form.

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \approx \dfrac{\rho_i^{n+1} - \rho_i^n}{\Delta t}, \quad \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x} \approx \dfrac{q_{i+1/2}^n - q_{i-1/2}^n}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to get the conservative update formula.

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = \rho_i^n - \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta x}\left(q_{i+1/2}^n - q_{i-1/2}^n\right)$$

**Step 4:** For the Godunov scheme, the inter-cell flux is the exact Riemann solver.

$$q_{i+1/2}^n = \mathcal{F}\left(\rho_i^n, \rho_{i+1}^n\right)$$

**Step 5:** LaxŌĆōFriedrichs uses a specific centered choice.

$$q_{i+1/2}^n = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[q(\rho_i^n) + q(\rho_{i+1}^n)\right] - \dfrac{\Delta x}{2\Delta t}\left(\rho_{i+1}^n - \rho_i^n\right)$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 2-lane urban arterial in Trivandrum follows the Greenshields model with $v_f = 60$ km/h and $\rho_j = 100$ veh/km/lane. At a signal turning green at $t = 0$, the upstream region has $\rho_L = 80$ veh/km/lane and the downstream region has $\rho_R = 20$ veh/km/lane. A discrete Godunov scheme with $\Delta x = 0.5$ km is used. Find: (a) the shock/rarefaction type and the analytical wave speed, (b) the maximum allowable time step $\Delta t$ for stability, and (c) the updated density at the interface cell after one time step.

### Part (a) ŌĆö Wave Structure and Analytical Speed

**Step 1:** Since $\rho_L = 80 > \rho_R = 20$, characteristics from the right overtake those from the left, forming a **shock wave**.

**Step 2:** Compute the flux on each side using $q(\rho) = v_f \rho (1 - \rho / \rho_j)$.

$$q_L = 60 \cdot 80 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{80}{100}\right) = 60 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.2 = 960 \text{ veh/hr}$$

$$q_R = 60 \cdot 20 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{20}{100}\right) = 60 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.8 = 960 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the RankineŌĆōHugoniot condition.

$$\dot{s} = \dfrac{q_R - q_L}{\rho_R - \rho_L} = \dfrac{960 - 960}{20 - 80} = \dfrac{0}{-60} = 0 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 4:** The shock is **stationary** ŌĆö this is the special case where the upstream flow exactly equals the downstream flow (a "contact discontinuity" in the fluid-dynamics sense). The high-density region will gradually discharge as vehicles accelerate.

### Part (b) ŌĆö Maximum Stable Time Step

**Step 1:** Compute the maximum characteristic speed for Greenshields.

$$\max \vert c(\rho) \vert = v_f = 60 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the CFL condition.

$$\Delta t \leq \dfrac{\Delta x}{v_f} = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ km}}{60 \text{ km/h}} = \dfrac{1}{120} \text{ h} = 0.5 \text{ min} = 30 \text{ s}$$

So the maximum stable time step is **$\Delta t_{max} = 30$ seconds**.

### Part (c) ŌĆö Godunov Update at the Interface

**Step 1:** Use the conservative Godunov formula at the interface cell $i$.

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = \rho_i^n - \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta x}\left(F^n_{i+1/2} - F^n_{i-1/2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Godunov flux. Since $\rho_L > \rho_R$ (shock case):

$$F = \min(q_L, q_R) = 960 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 3:** Assume the cell to the left has density $80$ and the cell to the right has density $20$, so the cell-center density is $\rho_i^n = 50$ veh/km (the average of the surrounding states).

$$q_i^n = 60 \cdot 50 \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{50}{100}\right) = 60 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.5 = 1500 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 4:** Use $\Delta t = \Delta x / v_f$ (the CFL-limit value, so the CFL number = 1).

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = 50 - \dfrac{1}{60}\left(960 - 960\right) = 50 \text{ veh/km}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation. As expected from the analytical result, the density at the interface cell is unchanged at first order, since the shock is stationary and the fluxes on both sides are balanced. The discontinuity persists; in higher-order schemes (e.g., MUSCL), the contact would slowly diffuse.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Numerical Schemes

| Property | LaxŌĆōFriedrichs | Godunov (Exact Riemann) | LaxŌĆōWendroff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order of accuracy | 1st in space & time | 1st in space & time | 2nd in space & time |
| Numerical diffusion | High | Low | Low |
| Dispersion errors | Minimal | Minimal | Yes (Gibbs phenomenon near shocks) |
| Captures shocks | Yes (smears them) | Yes (sharp) | No (oscillations) |
| Complexity to implement | Trivial | Moderate | Moderate |
| KTU 2024 likelihood | $\star\star\star$ | $\star\star\star\star\star$ | $\star\star$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Preference:** The Godunov scheme with the exact Riemann solver is the "gold standard" answer for traffic simulation problems in Module 4. Always write the flux function $\mathcal{F}(\rho_L, \rho_R)$ explicitly in your solution.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Traffic Simulation Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Road Geometry and Boundary Data] --> B[Define Greenshields or other Fundamental Diagram]
    B --> C[Set Initial Density Profile rho x 0]
    C --> D[Choose Numerical Scheme: Godunov or Lax Friedrichs]
    D --> E[Discretize Space Delta x and Time Delta t]
    E --> F[Apply CFL Stability Check]
    F --> G{Closed: CFL less than or equal to 1}
    G -- No --> H[Reduce Delta t and Recheck]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Compute Numerical Flux at each Cell Interface]
    I --> J[Update Density using Conservative Formula]
    J --> K{End of Simulation Time}
    K -- No --> I
    K -- Yes --> L[OUTPUT: Density rho x t, Velocity v x t, Flow q x t]
    L --> M[Visualize Shock Waves and Rarefaction Fans]
    L --> N[Compute Performance Metrics: Travel Time, Delay, Throughput]
```

## 4.2 Schematic ŌĆö The Fundamental Diagram ($q$ŌĆō$\rho$ Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin: rho equals 0, q equals 0] --> B[Free Flow Branch: rho small, v near v_f]
    B --> C[Critical Point: rho_c equals rho_j over 2, q equals q_max]
    C --> D[Forced Flow Branch: rho high, v decreasing]
    D --> E[Jam State: rho equals rho_j, q equals 0]
    F[Shock Path: chord from rho_L to rho_R] --> C
    G[Rarefaction Path: fan of characteristics] --> C
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Solution Architecture of a Traffic Simulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        IN1[Network Topology]
        IN2[Origin Destination Matrix]
        IN3[Signal Timing Plans]
    end
    subgraph MODEL_CORE
        M1[LWR Continuum Model]
        M2[Fundamental Diagram Library]
        M3[Numerical Solver: Godunov FVM]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        OUT1[Density Contour Plots]
        OUT2[Shock Wave Detection]
        OUT3[Level of Service Metrics]
    end
    IN1 --> M1
    IN2 --> M1
    IN3 --> M1
    M2 --> M1
    M1 --> M3
    M3 --> OUT1
    M3 --> OUT2
    M3 --> OUT3
```

## 4.4 Schematic ŌĆö Information Flow in a Godunov Update Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cell i-1 density at time n] --> C[Compute q at i-1 and i]
    B[Cell i density at time n] --> C
    D[Cell i+1 density at time n] --> C
    C --> E[Solve Riemann Problem at Interfaces]
    E --> F[Numerical Flux F at i minus 1 over 2 and i plus 1 over 2]
    F --> G[Update rho_i to the power n plus 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** Even if a question does not ask for a figure, a hand-drawn block diagram of the solver loop (Input ŌåÆ Discretize ŌåÆ CFL check ŌåÆ Flux ŌåÆ Update ŌåÆ Output) earns 1ŌĆō2 easy marks and demonstrates command over the methodology.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**State the LighthillŌĆōWhithamŌĆōRichards (LWR) continuity equation used in macroscopic traffic flow modelling. Define each term and mention the closure relation that makes the system solvable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The LWR equation expresses vehicle conservation on a roadway:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x} = 0$$

where:
- $\rho(x,t)$ = traffic density in vehicles per km,
- $q(x,t)$ = traffic flux in vehicles per hour,
- $x$ = position along the road, $t$ = time.

Since the equation has two unknowns, it requires a **constitutive relation** (fundamental diagram):

$$q = \rho \cdot v(\rho)$$

The most common choice is the **Greenshields model**:

$$v(\rho) = v_f\left(1 - \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_j}\right)$$

Substituting yields a closed nonlinear hyperbolic PDE solvable by finite difference methods.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**What is the fundamental diagram of traffic flow? Using the Greenshields model, derive the expression for the maximum flow (capacity) of a roadway.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **fundamental diagram** is a macroscopic relation between traffic flow $q$, density $\rho$, and speed $v$ under steady-state equilibrium conditions. For Greenshields:

$$v(\rho) = v_f\left(1 - \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_j}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad q(\rho) = v_f \rho - \dfrac{v_f}{\rho_j} \rho^2$$

To find capacity, set $dq/d\rho = 0$:

$$\dfrac{dq}{d\rho} = v_f - \dfrac{2 v_f}{\rho_j} \rho = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_c = \dfrac{\rho_j}{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$q_{max} = v_f \cdot \dfrac{\rho_j}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{v_f \rho_j}{4}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the LighthillŌĆōWhithamŌĆōRichards (LWR) continuity equation from the principle of vehicle conservation on a road segment of length $\Delta x$ during a time interval $\Delta t$. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-lane highway follows the Greenshields model with $v_f = 80$ km/h and $\rho_j = 150$ veh/km. A LaxŌĆōFriedrichs finite difference scheme is used with $\Delta x = 1$ km. The density profile at time $t^n$ across three consecutive cells is $\rho_{i-1}^n = 30$, $\rho_i^n = 50$, $\rho_{i+1}^n = 70$ veh/km. Compute the updated density $\rho_i^{n+1}$ using the maximum stable time step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### Solution to A(a) ŌĆö Derivation of LWR (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup.** Consider a road segment extending from $x$ to $x + \Delta x$ during the interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$. Let $\rho(x,t)$ denote vehicle density (veh/km) and $q(x,t)$ denote flux (veh/hr).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Conservation of vehicles.** The number of vehicles in the segment at time $t$ is $\rho(x,t)\Delta x$. The change over $\Delta t$ must equal the net inflow:

$$\left[\rho(x, t+\Delta t) - \rho(x,t)\right] \Delta x = \left[q(x,t) - q(x+\Delta x, t)\right] \Delta t$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rearrange.**

$$\dfrac{\rho(x, t+\Delta t) - \rho(x,t)}{\Delta t} + \dfrac{q(x+\Delta x, t) - q(x, t)}{\Delta x} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Take limits $\Delta t \to 0$ and $\Delta x \to 0$.**

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial x} = 0 \qquad \text{(LWR continuity equation)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Assumptions made.**
- Vehicles are treated as a continuum (macroscopic view).
- The road has no entries or exits in the segment.
- All drivers instantaneously adopt the equilibrium speed for the local density.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Closure.** A fundamental diagram $q = \rho \cdot v(\rho)$ closes the system. Substituting gives the **nonlinear conservation law**:

$$\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{dq}{d\rho} \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \text{where } \dfrac{dq}{d\rho} = v_f\left(1 - \dfrac{2\rho}{\rho_j}\right)$$

### Solution to A(b) ŌĆö LaxŌĆōFriedrichs Update (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify the CFL-stable time step.**

For Greenshields, the maximum wave speed is $v_f = 80$ km/h. The CFL condition gives:

$$\Delta t \leq \dfrac{\Delta x}{v_f} = \dfrac{1 \text{ km}}{80 \text{ km/h}} = \dfrac{1}{80} \text{ h} = 0.0125 \text{ h} = 45 \text{ s}$$

So the **maximum stable time step is $\Delta t_{max} = 45$ s**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the flux at cells $i-1$ and $i+1$ using Greenshields.**

$$q(\rho) = v_f \rho \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho}{\rho_j}\right) = 80\rho - \dfrac{80}{150}\rho^2$$

$$q_{i-1}^n = 80(30) - \dfrac{80}{150}(30)^2 = 2400 - 480 = 1920 \text{ veh/hr}$$

$$q_{i+1}^n = 80(70) - \dfrac{80}{150}(70)^2 = 5600 - 2613.33 = 2986.67 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the LaxŌĆōFriedrichs formula.**

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\rho_{i+1}^n + \rho_{i-1}^n\right) - \dfrac{\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(q_{i+1}^n - q_{i-1}^n\right)$$

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = \dfrac{1}{2}(70 + 30) - \dfrac{1}{160}\left(2986.67 - 1920\right)$$

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = 50 - \dfrac{1066.67}{160} = 50 - 6.667$$

$$\boxed{\rho_i^{n+1} \approx 43.33 \text{ veh/km}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation.** The density at cell $i$ has decreased from 50 to 43.33 veh/km. Physically, this represents vehicles moving away from the centre cell as the upstream region is more congested than the downstream. The LaxŌĆōFriedrichs scheme has smoothed out the gradient ŌĆö note that this scheme is **inherently diffusive**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Godunov scheme** for solving the LWR equation. Derive the formula for the numerical flux at a cell interface in terms of the left and right Riemann states $\rho_L$ and $\rho_R$ for the **Greenshields fundamental diagram**. State the conditions under which a shock or rarefaction occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signalized intersection releases a platoon of vehicles into a road with $v_f = 50$ km/h, $\rho_j = 100$ veh/km. At $t = 0$, the upstream density is $\rho_L = 90$ veh/km and the downstream density is $\rho_R = 10$ veh/km. Compute: (i) the analytical shock speed using RankineŌĆōHugoniot, (ii) the Godunov numerical flux at the interface, and (iii) the position of the shock after 5 minutes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### Solution to B(a) ŌĆö Godunov Scheme and Flux Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö The Godunov scheme is a finite-volume method** that updates the cell-averaged density using a conservative formula:

$$\rho_i^{n+1} = \rho_i^n - \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta x}\left(F^n_{i+1/2} - F^n_{i-1/2}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö The numerical flux $F^n_{i+1/2}$ is the flux crossing the cell interface at $x_{i+1/2}$ during time step $n$.** It is obtained by solving the **Riemann problem** with left state $\rho_L = \rho_i^n$ and right state $\rho_R = \rho_{i+1}^n$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö For the Greenshields fundamental diagram** $q(\rho) = v_f \rho (1 - \rho/\rho_j)$, the $q$ŌĆō$\rho$ curve is a concave downward parabola.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Three cases for the Riemann flux:**

- If $\rho_L < \rho_R$ (i.e., lighter upstream, denser downstream), characteristics converge and a **shock** forms. The flux is:
$$F = \min\left[q(\rho_L), q(\rho_R)\right]$$

- If $\rho_L > \rho_R$ (denser upstream, lighter downstream), characteristics diverge and a **rarefaction fan** forms. The flux is the value at the dip of the $q$ŌĆō$\rho$ curve:
$$F = q(\rho_c) = q_{max} = \dfrac{v_f \rho_j}{4}$$

- If $\rho_L = \rho_R$, there is no discontinuity and $F = q(\rho_L)$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compact Godunov flux expression:**

$$F(\rho_L, \rho_R) = \begin{cases} \min\left[q(\rho_L), q(\rho_R)\right], & \rho_L \leq \rho_R \\[4pt] \dfrac{v_f \rho_j}{4}, & \rho_L > \rho_R \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö The scheme is first-order accurate, conservative, and shock-capturing.** It satisfies the entropy condition (no unphysical expansion shocks).

### Solution to B(b) ŌĆö Signal Release Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute fluxes using Greenshields** $q(\rho) = 50\rho - 0.5\rho^2$.

$$q_L = 50(90) - 0.5(90)^2 = 4500 - 4050 = 450 \text{ veh/hr}$$

$$q_R = 50(10) - 0.5(10)^2 = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Analytical shock speed (RankineŌĆōHugoniot).**

Since $\rho_L = 90 > \rho_R = 10$, a shock forms. The shock speed is:

$$s = \dfrac{q_R - q_L}{\rho_R - \rho_L} = \dfrac{450 - 450}{10 - 90} = \dfrac{0}{-80} = 0 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Godunov numerical flux at the interface.**

Since $\rho_L > \rho_R$, this is a rarefaction case. The Godunov flux is the capacity:

$$F = \dfrac{v_f \rho_j}{4} = \dfrac{50 \times 100}{4} = 1250 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Position of the shock after 5 minutes.**

Since $s = 0$, the shock remains at the original location of the discontinuity (the signal stop-line). After $t = 5$ min = $1/12$ h:

$$x_{shock}(t) = x_0 + s \cdot t = x_0 + 0 = x_0$$

The shock is **stationary** and remains at the signal. Physically, the upstream queue length is sustained because the discharge flow at the signal equals the upstream flow demand ŌĆö a classic bottleneck condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** When discharge flow from a signal equals the upstream demand, the queue never clears. Traffic engineers remedy this by increasing the **green time** (which raises discharge flow) or by **metering** the upstream demand.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the closure relation.** Writing only the LWR equation without specifying $q = \rho v(\rho)$ will cost you at least **1 mark** out of 14.
> 2. **Confusing the Greenshields parabola with the Underwood or Greenberg models.** Stay with Greenshields unless the problem states otherwise ŌĆö it is by far the most common in KTU papers.
> 3. **Sign errors in shock speed.** Always use $\dot{s} = (q_R - q_L)/(\rho_R - \rho_L)$. A positive value means the shock moves to the right.
> 4. **Ignoring the CFL condition.** Any finite difference update without a CFL check is incomplete. Examiners explicitly look for this stability check.
> 5. **Mixing up the Godunov flux cases.** Memorize: $\rho_L \leq \rho_R \Rightarrow$ shock (use min); $\rho_L > \rho_R \Rightarrow$ rarefaction (use $q_{max}$ for Greenshields).
> 6. **Unit inconsistency.** Convert $v_f$ in km/h to consistent units (km/h with $x$ in km and $t$ in h) before any numerical computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö print this and revise 1 hour before the exam.**

- **Core PDE:** $\partial_t \rho + \partial_x q = 0$ with closure $q = \rho v(\rho)$.
- **Greenshields speedŌĆōdensity:** $v = v_f(1 - \rho / \rho_j)$ ŌĆö linear in $\rho$.
- **Greenshields flux:** $q = v_f \rho - (v_f / \rho_j) \rho^2$ ŌĆö quadratic (parabolic) in $\rho$.
- **Capacity (max flow):** $q_{max} = v_f \rho_j / 4$ at $\rho_c = \rho_j/2$, $v_c = v_f/2$.
- **Characteristic speed:** $c(\rho) = dq/d\rho = v_f(1 - 2\rho / \rho_j)$ ŌĆö ranges from $+v_f$ (empty) to $-v_f$ (jammed).
- **Shock wave speed (RankineŌĆōHugoniot):** $\dot{s} = (q_R - q_L)/(\rho_R - \rho_L)$ ŌĆö geometrically the slope of the chord.
- **Two wave structures:** rarefaction fan (when $\rho_L < \rho_R$) and shock (when $\rho_L > \rho_R$).
- **Godunov scheme (favoured):** conservative update with exact Riemann flux; the flux is $\min(q_L, q_R)$ for shocks and $q_{max}$ for rarefactions.
- **LaxŌĆōFriedrichs scheme (backup):** simple centered formula, very diffusive, always stable under CFL.
- **CFL stability:** $\Delta t \leq \Delta x / v_f$ for Greenshields ŌĆö non-negotiable for explicit schemes.
- **Macroscopic vs microscopic:** Macroscopic = continuum PDE; Microscopic = car-following/NagelŌĆōSchreckenberg cellular automata.
- **Engineering uses:** signal timing, evacuation planning, ITS, CAV control, bottleneck analysis.
- **Always convert units** of $v_f$, $\rho_j$, $\Delta x$, $\Delta t$ to a consistent system before computing.
- **Mention the assumptions:** continuum approximation, equilibrium speed, no on/off-ramps (unless stated).
- **Draw a figure** (block diagram or $q$ŌĆō$\rho$ curve) wherever possible ŌĆö it earns easy methodology marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weather Prediction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET417 - NUMERICAL METHODS FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weather Prediction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP)** is the science and practice of predicting the state of the atmosphere ŌĆö temperature, pressure, wind velocity, humidity, and precipitation ŌĆö by solving the **primitive equations** (the system of non-linear partial differential equations governing atmospheric motion) on a discrete spatial grid using numerical methods, starting from observed initial conditions.

In the KTU 2024 PECET417 framework, *weather prediction* is studied as a **canonical real-world application of numerical analysis** because it simultaneously exercises:
- Solution of **Initial Value Problems (IVPs)** for Ordinary Differential Equations (e.g., time integration).
- Discretization of **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** in space.
- Concepts of **stability, consistency, and convergence** (Lax equivalence theorem).
- Sensitivity to initial conditions (**chaos theory** ŌĆö Lorenz system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** Numerical Weather Prediction is the numerical solution of the governing atmospheric equations (primitive equations) using finite-difference, finite-volume, or spectral methods on a 3-D grid covering the globe, to forecast future atmospheric states from current observations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

Imagine the entire Earth's atmosphere sliced like a **3-D checkerboard** of cubes (each cube ~10 km wide, ~100 m thick, ~50 layers vertically). Inside every cube you track five numbers: temperature, pressure, eastward wind $u$, northward wind $v$, vertical wind $w$, and humidity $q$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Observe:** Place weather balloons, satellites, and ocean buoys to read those five numbers in every cube **right now**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Physics:** Each cube obeys Newton's laws (momentum), the gas law (pressure-density-temperature), conservation of mass (continuity), and conservation of energy (thermodynamics). These are six coupled equations per cube.

**Step 3 ŌĆö March Forward in Time:** Using a small time step (say **╬öt = 600 s = 10 minutes**), the computer nudges all the numbers forward by **╬öt** using finite-difference formulas (Euler, Runge-Kutta, Leapfrog).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Repeat:** ~144 times per simulated day ŌåÆ 10-day forecast takes ~1,440 steps ├Ś millions of cubes = billions of arithmetic operations.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Repeat Globally:** The whole 3-D globe marches forward, producing tomorrow's, next week's, or next month's atmospheric state.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Butterfly Effect Connection:** Edward Lorenz (1963) discovered that a change of 0.0001 in initial conditions produces dramatically different forecasts after ~5 days. This is why weather forecasts are inherently probabilistic beyond **~10 days** ŌĆö a key numerical-methods lesson on **ill-conditioning and sensitivity**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Radius of Earth | $R_E$ | **6,371 km** |
| Surface gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s┬▓** |
| Gas constant for dry air | $R_d$ | **287.058 J/(kg┬ĘK)** |
| Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | **1004.5 J/(kg┬ĘK)** |
| Coriolis parameter (mid-latitude) | $f$ | **~10Ōü╗Ōü┤ sŌü╗┬╣** |
| Typical horizontal grid spacing | $\Delta x$ | **10ŌĆō25 km (global), 1ŌĆō4 km (regional)** |
| Typical time step | $\Delta t$ | **60ŌĆō900 s** |
| Forecast horizon | ŌĆö | **1ŌĆō16 days** |
| Forecast skill (useful accuracy) | ŌĆö | **~7ŌĆō10 days** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Primitive Equations ŌĆö Foundation of NWP

The **primitive equations** are the seven fundamental governing equations of the atmosphere. They form a closed system of **non-linear PDEs** in seven unknowns: $u, v, w$ (wind components), $T$ (temperature), $p$ (pressure), $\rho$ (density), and $q$ (specific humidity).

### 2.1.1 The Seven Primitive Equations

1. **Continuity (mass conservation) equation:**
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

2. **Momentum equation in x-direction (zonal):**
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - fv + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = 0
$$

3. **Momentum equation in y-direction (meridional):**
$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + fu + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = 0
$$

4. **Hydrostatic equation (vertical momentum, valid for synoptic-scale motion):**
$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\rho g
$$

5. **Thermodynamic energy equation:**
$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial T}{\partial y} + w\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial z} + \frac{g}{c_p}\right) = \frac{Q}{c_p}
$$
where $Q$ is the diabatic heating rate.

6. **Equation of state (ideal gas law for moist air):**
$$
p = \rho R_d T (1 + 0.61\, q)
$$

7. **Moisture continuity equation:**
$$
\frac{\partial q}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial q}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial q}{\partial z} = S_q
$$
where $S_q$ represents sources/sinks (evaporation, condensation, precipitation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Primitive"?** They are the *most fundamental* (primal) equations. They make no simplifications about the basic physical processes. The term distinguishes them from simplified "filtered" models (e.g., the barotropic vorticity equation) that suppress certain wave types.

## 2.2 Discretization Strategy ŌĆö From Continuous to Discrete

### 2.2.1 Spatial Discretization

Continuous fields $u(x,y,z,t)$ are replaced by discrete values $u_{i,j,k}^{(n)}$ at grid points $(x_i, y_j, z_k)$ and time level $t^{(n)} = t_0 + n\,\Delta t$.

Three common spatial schemes:
- **Finite Difference Method (FDM):** Direct Taylor expansion of derivatives.
- **Finite Volume Method (FVM):** Conserves mass/energy exactly.
- **Spectral Method:** Represents fields as truncated spherical harmonics (used in European Centre ECMWF model).

### 2.2.2 Time Integration Schemes

| Scheme | Update Rule | Order | Stability (CFL) | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Euler** | $u^{n+1} = u^n + \Delta t \cdot F(u^n)$ | 1 | Very strict | Pedagogical only |
| **Leapfrog (centered)** | $u^{n+1} = u^{n-1} + 2\Delta t \cdot F(u^n)$ | 2 | Moderate | Classical NWP |
| **Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4)** | 4-stage evaluation | 4 | Stricter | Modern high-res models |
| **Semi-implicit** | Implicit treatment of fast waves | 1ŌĆō2 | Very stable | ECMWF, GFS |
| **Split-explicit** | Different $\Delta t$ per process | 1 | Stable | WRF, MPAS |

### 2.2.3 The CFL (CourantŌĆōFriedrichsŌĆōLewy) Stability Condition

For the 1-D linear advection equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$, an explicit time-integration scheme is **stable only if**:
$$
C = \frac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq C_{\max}
$$
where $C$ is the **Courant number** and $C_{\max}$ depends on the scheme:
- Forward Euler: $C_{\max} = 1$
- Leapfrog: $C_{\max} = 1$
- RK4: $C_{\max} \approx 2.83$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of CFL:** Information (e.g., a wind gust) must not travel more than one grid cell $\Delta x$ in one time step $\Delta t$. If $c\,\Delta t > \Delta x$, the simulation "skips over" a cell and the wave is missed ŌåÆ instability / numerical noise.

## 2.3 The Lorenz System ŌĆö Chaos and Predictability Limit

The **Lorenz equations** (1963) are a 3-variable simplified model of thermal convection in the atmosphere, but they capture the essence of weather chaos:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \sigma(y - x)
$$
$$
\frac{dy}{dt} = x(\rho - z) - y
$$
$$
\frac{dz}{dt} = xy - \beta z
$$
with the canonical parameters: $\sigma = 10$, $\rho = 28$, $\beta = 8/3$.

Key results:
- Trajectories lie on the **Lorenz attractor** ŌĆö a fractal, butterfly-shaped set in 3-D phase space.
- **Sensitivity:** Two initial conditions differing by $10^{-8}$ diverge exponentially with Lyapunov time $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1}$ where $\lambda_1 \approx 0.906$ for these parameters ŌåÆ $\tau \approx 1.1$ time units.
- **Practical limit of deterministic weather forecasting: ~2 weeks.**

## 2.4 Data Assimilation ŌĆö Bridging Observations and Models

Before forecasting can begin, observational data (from radiosondes, satellites, radar, ships, aircraft) must be merged with a model **first guess** to produce the best possible **initial conditions**. Two main methods:

- **3D-Var:** Minimize $J(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^b)^T \mathbf{B}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^b) + \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{y} - H(\mathbf{x}))^T \mathbf{R}^{-1} (\mathbf{y} - H(\mathbf{x}))$ at a single time.
- **4D-Var:** Same as 3D-Var but the model is integrated within the cost function over an assimilation window ŌĆö the operational standard at ECMWF.

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ = state vector, $\mathbf{x}^b$ = background (first guess), $\mathbf{B}$ = background-error covariance.
- $\mathbf{y}$ = observations, $H$ = observation operator, $\mathbf{R}$ = observation-error covariance.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas needed for KTU Module 4 weather-prediction problems are summarized below. **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells** ŌĆö absolute value is rendered as `\vert`.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1-D Linear Advection (PDE) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ | $c$ = wave speed (m/s) |
| 2 | Forward-time central-space (FTCS) explicit | $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n)$ | **Unconditionally unstable** |
| 3 | Leapfrog scheme (2nd order, time) | $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n-1} - \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n)$ | Courant $\vert C \vert \le 1$ |
| 4 | Lax-Friedrichs scheme | $u_i^{n+1} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i+1}^n + u_{i-1}^n) - \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n)$ | Stable for $\vert C \vert \le 1$ |
| 5 | Courant number | $C = \dfrac{\vert c \vert\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | CFL stability bound (leapfrog) | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | $c\,\Delta t \le \Delta x$ |
| 7 | Maximum stable time step | $\Delta t_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\vert c \vert_{\max}}$ | Used to choose $\Delta t$ |
| 8 | Truncation error (FTCS) | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t, \Delta x^2)$ | First order in time |
| 9 | Truncation error (Leapfrog) | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2, \Delta x^2)$ | Second order in both |
| 10 | Hydrostatic approximation | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\rho g$ | Valid for $L \gg H$ |
| 11 | Coriolis parameter | $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$ | $\Omega = 7.2921\times10^{-5}$ rad/s |
| 12 | Gas constant (dry air) | $R_d = 287.058$ J/(kg┬ĘK) | Used in $p = \rho R_d T$ |
| 13 | Lorenz system | $\dot{x} = \sigma(y-x),\; \dot{y} = x(\rho - z) - y,\; \dot{z} = xy - \beta z$ | $\sigma = 10,\; \rho = 28,\; \beta = 8/3$ |
| 14 | Lyapunov time (Lorenz) | $\tau \approx 1 / \lambda_{\max}$ | $\lambda_{\max} \approx 0.906$ |
| 15 | 3D-Var cost function | $J(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^b)^T\mathbf{B}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^b) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{y}-H\mathbf{x})^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}(\mathbf{y}-H\mathbf{x})$ | Minimize ŌåÆ optimal $\mathbf{x}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Scientific Utility

NWP is the **bedrock of modern meteorology** with downstream impact on:
- **Aviation safety** (turbulence, icing, jet stream routing).
- **Agriculture** (frost, drought, planting decisions).
- **Disaster preparedness** (cyclone, flood, heat-wave warnings).
- **Renewable energy** (wind-farm output, solar irradiance).
- **Military operations** (visibility, sea state).
- **Climate science** (NWP models doubled as early climate models).

It is also a **textbook example** in numerical-analysis pedagogy because it stresses:
- High dimensionality (~10Ōü╣ unknowns).
- Non-linearity ŌåÆ iterative solvers, NewtonŌĆōKrylov methods.
- Stiffness ŌåÆ implicit/semi-implicit time stepping.
- Validation against observations ŌåÆ root-mean-square error, anomaly correlation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Finite-Difference Approximation of the Advection Equation

We start with the **1-D linear advection equation** ŌĆö the simplest PDE that captures the transport of an atmospheric property (e.g., pollutant concentration) by a constant wind of speed $c$:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Discretize the domain

Introduce a uniform grid with spacing $\Delta x$ in space and time step $\Delta t$. The continuous function $u(x,t)$ is replaced by discrete values $u_i^n$ where:
- $i$ indexes spatial location: $x_i = i\,\Delta x$
- $n$ indexes time: $t^n = n\,\Delta t$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Taylor-expand $u_i^{n+1}$ in time

Expand $u(x_i, t^{n+1}) = u(x_i, t^n + \Delta t)$ about $(x_i, t^n)$:

$$
u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n + \Delta t\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)_i^n + \frac{(\Delta t)^2}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}\right)_i^n + \frac{(\Delta t)^3}{6}\left(\frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial t^3}\right)_i^n + \cdots
$$

Solving for the time derivative:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)_i^n = \frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} - \frac{\Delta t}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}\right)_i^n - \mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2)
$$

Therefore the **forward-time finite-difference** approximation is:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right)_i^n \approx \frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta t)
$$

This truncation error is **first-order accurate in time**.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Taylor-expand $u_{i+1}^n$ and $u_{i-1}^n$ in space

Forward expansion:
$$
u_{i+1}^n = u_i^n + \Delta x\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)_i^n + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^n + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6}\left(\frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\right)_i^n + \cdots
$$

Backward expansion:
$$
u_{i-1}^n = u_i^n - \Delta x\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)_i^n + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^n - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6}\left(\frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\right)_i^n + \cdots
$$

Subtracting the second from the first (forward ŌłÆ backward) gives the **central-space derivative**:

$$
u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n = 2\Delta x\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)_i^n + \frac{2(\Delta x)^3}{6}\left(\frac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}\right)_i^n + \cdots
$$

Solving for the spatial derivative:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)_i^n = \frac{u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n}{2\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
$$

This is **second-order accurate in space**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the PDE

Plug the two finite-difference approximations into the original PDE:

$$
\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} + c\left[\frac{u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n}{2\Delta x}\right] \approx 0
$$

Rearrange to obtain the **Forward-Time Central-Space (FTCS)** scheme:

$$
\boxed{\,u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n\right)\,}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Stability analysis (von Neumann)

Assume a Fourier mode solution: $u_i^n = G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k i \Delta x}$ where $G$ is the **amplification factor** and $k$ is the wavenumber.

Substitute into the FTCS formula:

$$
G^{n+1} e^{\mathrm{i} k i \Delta x} = G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k i \Delta x} - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left[G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k (i+1)\Delta x} - G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k (i-1)\Delta x}\right]
$$

Divide both sides by $G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k i \Delta x}$:

$$
G = 1 - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(e^{\mathrm{i} k \Delta x} - e^{-\mathrm{i} k \Delta x}\right)
$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{\mathrm{i}\theta} - e^{-\mathrm{i}\theta} = 2\mathrm{i}\sin\theta$:

$$
G = 1 - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(2\mathrm{i}\sin(k\Delta x)\right)
$$

$$
G = 1 - \mathrm{i}\,C\,\sin(k\Delta x)
$$

where $C = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}$ is the **Courant number**.

The **modulus squared** of $G$ is:

$$
\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2 \sin^2(k\Delta x)
$$

Since $\sin^2(k\Delta x) > 0$ for any non-zero $k$, we have $\vert G \vert^2 > 1$ for **any** $\Delta t > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** $\vert G \vert > 1$ for all non-trivial modes ŌåÆ **FTCS is UNCONDITIONALLY UNSTABLE** for the linear advection equation. This is why the FTCS scheme is **NEVER used in NWP** for advection. The leapfrog or Lax-Friedrichs scheme is preferred.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö CFL Stability Condition for the Leapfrog Scheme

The leapfrog scheme for advection is:

$$
u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n-1} - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}\left(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n\right)
$$

Substitute $u_i^n = G^n e^{\mathrm{i} k i \Delta x}$:

$$
G = G^{-1} - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}\left(e^{\mathrm{i} k \Delta x} - e^{-\mathrm{i} k \Delta x}\right)
$$

$$
G = G^{-1} - 2\mathrm{i}\,C\,\sin(k\Delta x)
$$

Multiply by $G$:

$$
G^2 + 2\mathrm{i}\,C\,\sin(k\Delta x)\,G - 1 = 0
$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $G$**. The roots are:

$$
G = -\mathrm{i}\,C\,\sin(k\Delta x) \pm \sqrt{1 - C^2\sin^2(k\Delta x)}
$$

For **stability**, both roots must satisfy $\vert G \vert \le 1$. Analyzing this requires $\vert C \vert \le 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFL Condition for Leapfrog:** The leapfrog scheme is stable if and only if
> $$
> \boxed{\,\left\vert C \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x} \right\vert \le 1\,}
> $$
> Equivalently, $\Delta t \le \dfrac{\Delta x}{\vert c \vert_{\max}}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Numerical Worked Example (Lax-Friedrichs)

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + 2\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ on $0 \le x \le 1$ with initial condition $u(x,0) = \sin(2\pi x)$ using the Lax-Friedrichs scheme with $\Delta x = 0.1$ and $\Delta t = 0.04$. Compute $u$ at $t = 0.08$ for $x = 0.3$ (i.e., at $i=3$, $n=2$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Courant number:**
$$
C = \frac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x} = \frac{2 \times 0.04}{0.1} = 0.8
$$
$\vert C \vert = 0.8 \le 1$ ŌåÆ scheme is stable.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Initial condition (n = 0):**
At $x_i = i \times 0.1$:
- $u_0^0 = \sin(0) = 0$
- $u_1^0 = \sin(0.2\pi) = 0.5878$
- $u_2^0 = \sin(0.4\pi) = 0.9511$
- $u_3^0 = \sin(0.6\pi) = 0.9511$
- $u_4^0 = \sin(0.8\pi) = 0.5878$
- $u_5^0 = \sin(\pi) = 0$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Lax-Friedrichs scheme (n = 0 ŌåÆ 1):**

The scheme is:
$$
u_i^{n+1} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i+1}^n + u_{i-1}^n) - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}(u_{i+1}^n - u_{i-1}^n)
$$

At $i = 3$:
$$
u_3^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_4^0 + u_2^0) - \frac{0.8}{2}(u_4^0 - u_2^0)
$$
$$
u_3^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.5878 + 0.9511) - 0.4 \times (0.5878 - 0.9511)
$$
$$
u_3^1 = 0.7695 - 0.4 \times (-0.3633)
$$
$$
u_3^1 = 0.7695 + 0.1453 = 0.9148
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the scheme again (n = 1 ŌåÆ 2), still at $i = 3$:**

We need $u_4^1$ and $u_2^1$ as well.

At $i = 4$ (n = 0 ŌåÆ 1):
$$
u_4^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_5^0 + u_3^0) - 0.4 \times (u_5^0 - u_3^0)
$$
$$
u_4^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0 + 0.9511) - 0.4 \times (0 - 0.9511)
$$
$$
u_4^1 = 0.4755 + 0.3804 = 0.8560
$$

At $i = 2$ (n = 0 ŌåÆ 1):
$$
u_2^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_3^0 + u_1^0) - 0.4 \times (u_3^0 - u_1^0)
$$
$$
u_2^1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.9511 + 0.5878) - 0.4 \times (0.9511 - 0.5878)
$$
$$
u_2^1 = 0.7695 - 0.4 \times 0.3633 = 0.7695 - 0.1453 = 0.6241
$$

Now at $i = 3$, $n = 2$:
$$
u_3^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_4^1 + u_2^1) - 0.4 \times (u_4^1 - u_2^1)
$$
$$
u_3^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.8560 + 0.6241) - 0.4 \times (0.8560 - 0.6241)
$$
$$
u_3^2 = 0.7400 - 0.4 \times 0.2319
$$
$$
u_3^2 = 0.7400 - 0.0928 = 0.6473
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Exact solution for verification:**

The exact solution is $u(x,t) = \sin(2\pi(x - ct))$.

At $x = 0.3, t = 0.08, c = 2$:
$$
u_{\text{exact}}(0.3, 0.08) = \sin(2\pi(0.3 - 0.16)) = \sin(2\pi \times 0.14) = \sin(0.8796) = 0.7711
$$

**Numerical error:** $\vert u_{\text{exact}} - u_{\text{computed}} \vert = \vert 0.7711 - 0.6473 \vert = 0.1238$ ŌĆö about **16%**. Acceptable for such a coarse grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Halving $\Delta x$ and $\Delta t$ (with $C$ fixed) typically reduces the Lax-Friedrichs error by a factor of 2 (first-order accuracy). Production NWP uses **second-order** schemes (e.g., leapfrog, Lax-Wendroff) to keep accuracy high without extreme grid refinement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparing Numerical Schemes for NWP Advection

| Scheme | Time order | Space order | Stability (CFL) | Computational cost/cell | Numerical diffusion | Use in NWP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Euler | 1 | ŌĆö | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | Low | High | No |
| FTCS | 1 | 2 | **Unstable** | Low | ŌĆö | **Never** |
| Lax-Friedrichs | 1 | 1 | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | Low | High | No (educational) |
| Leapfrog | 2 | 2 | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | Medium (needs 2 time levels) | Low | Yes (classical) |
| Lax-Wendroff | 2 | 2 | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | Medium | LowŌĆōmoderate | Yes (mesoscale) |
| Upwind (1st order) | 1 | 1 | $\vert C \vert \le 1$ | Low | High | Yes (robust) |
| Semi-implicit | 1ŌĆō2 | 2 | Very lenient | High (matrix solve) | Low | Yes (ECMWF, GFS) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The NWP Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the complete NWP pipeline from observations to forecast delivery. All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obsStart([Real-world atmosphere]) --> obsA[Observations: radiosondes satellites radar buoys aircraft ships]
    obsA --> obsB[Quality control: outlier rejection bias correction]
    obsB --> da1[Data Assimilation: 3D-Var or 4D-Var]
    da1 --> da2[Optimal initial conditions x0 on 3D grid]
    da2 --> model1[NWP model: primitive equations discretized]
    model1 --> model2{Time integration scheme}
    model2 --> model3[Spatial discretization: finite difference or spectral]
    model3 --> model4[Physics parameterizations: radiation convection clouds]
    model4 --> model5[Time stepping with CFL-respecting dt]
    model5 --> fcst1[Forecast fields at t plus 1 to plus 384 hours]
    fcst1 --> fcst2[Post-processing: bias correction downscaling]
    fcst2 --> fcst3([Public weather forecast products])
    fcst1 --> ens[Ensemble: perturbed ICs and physics]
    ens --> ens1[Probabilistic forecast skill and uncertainty]
    ens1 --> fcst3
```

## 4.2 Lorenz Attractor ŌĆö Phase-Space Trajectory

The following block diagram describes the qualitative structure of the Lorenz attractor, illustrating how a deterministic system can produce chaotic, non-repeating trajectories that nonetheless remain bounded.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial condition x0 y0 z0] --> B[Evaluate sigma y minus x dot x]
    A --> C[Evaluate x rho minus z minus y dot y]
    A --> D[Evaluate xy minus beta z dot z]
    B --> E[Update x y z via RK4 with dt]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Test: stop at t equals 100}
    F -->|no| E
    F -->|yes| G[Plot trajectory: butterfly shaped attractor in 3D phase space]
    G --> H[Compute Lyapunov exponent lambda 1: positive 0.906 indicates chaos]
    H --> I[Conclusion: weather forecast skill is fundamentally limited to about 10 to 14 days]
```

## 4.3 Arakawa Staggered Grid (Schematic Block Diagram)

The Arakawa C-grid is the most widely used horizontal staggering in NWP because it improves the representation of inertia-gravity waves. The following block-level diagram maps the placement of variables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    grid1[Cell corner: pressure p and temperature T] --> grid2[East edge: zonal wind u]
    grid1 --> grid3[North edge: meridional wind v]
    grid2 --> grid4[Mass flux: rho times u on east face]
    grid3 --> grid5[Mass flux: rho times v on north face]
    grid4 --> grid6[Continuity equation: d rho dt plus divergence equals 0]
    grid5 --> grid6
    grid6 --> grid7[Compute u at cell faces: central differencing]
    grid6 --> grid8[Compute v at cell faces: central differencing]
    grid7 --> grid9[Update momentum equation: du dt equals minus grad p over rho plus Coriolis]
    grid8 --> grid9
    grid9 --> grid10([Forecast: next time level with CFL dt])
```

## 4.4 Stability Triangle ŌĆö von Neumann Analysis

The following block diagram explains the von Neumann stability workflow for any explicit time-integration scheme applied to a linear PDE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pde[Start: linear PDE with constant coefficients] --> a[Assume Fourier mode: u equals G to the n times exp i k x]
    a --> b[Substitute into finite-difference scheme]
    b --> c[Solve algebraic equation for amplification factor G of k]
    c --> d[Compute modulus squared of G]
    d --> e{Is modulus of G less than or equal to 1 for all k}
    e -->|yes| f[Scheme is stable: choose dt to satisfy bound]
    e -->|no| g[Scheme is unstable: redesign or use implicit method]
    f --> h[CFL condition: extract dt max as function of dx and wave speed]
    g --> h
    h --> i([Implement scheme in NWP code])
```
